segments.s670-g425: This is a coordinated noun phrase. گرازنده is an agent-noun/attributive ('boar-hunter' or 'boar-slayer') modifying the following proper name بهرام: 'Bahram the boar-hunter.' و is the simple coordinator 'and' linking that phrase to تازنده گور, where تازنده is a present-participial adjective ('rushing, fleet') modifying گور; together تازنده گور = 'the rushing/galloping onager.' There is no finite verb here; these are nominal phrases enumerated before the next clause.
segments.s670-g426: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic z-). گرمای آن دشت is a genitive phrase 'the heat of that plain' (گرمای = 'the heat of ...'); تفسیده is a past-participial adjective 'withered/decayed' used predicatively, and هور is the noun being described ('splendor/light' here, not a proper name). The clause omits an explicit copula (a common poetic Persian pattern), so the line reads literally 'From the heat of that plain, the splendour had withered.' The ordering places the causal prepositional phrase before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s670-g385: جهاندار شاهست is a nominal statement where جهاندار ('lord of the world') serves as predicate and شاهست is an older fused form of شاه است ('is king'), so the phrase reads 'the king is the world‑lord' or more naturally 'the king is the sovereign.' The conjunction وما بنده‌ایم follows, with وما 'and we' as subject and بنده‌ایم the present indicative/copula construction 'we are servants' (بنده + -ایم), so the two clauses are joined by و.
segments.s670-g386: دل و جان is a paired noun phrase 'heart and life' (دل = heart, جان = life/soul). به مهر وی is a prepositional phrase 'with/for his love' (به + مهر + وی), and آگنده‌ایم is a predicative adjective with the first‑person plural copula '-ایم', literally 'we are filled' (آگنده + -ایم) — so the whole segment means 'our heart and life are filled with love for him.'
segments.s670-g387: The clause has an interrogative چـه 'what' at the front, followed by the verb بینید (here used in the sense 'see') and then the subject بینندگان 'the onlookers/spectators' and the locative اندرین 'in this / among these.' Persian allows verb–subject order in poetry, so this reads 'What do the spectators see in this?'
segments.s670-g388: This is another interrogative clause beginning with چه 'what'. گوییم here functions as a first‑person plural verb form 'we say' or 'shall we say' (poetic subjunctive/indicative sense), followed by the preposition با 'to/with' and شهریار زمین 'the king/sovereign of the land.' Word order is Subject omitted (we) – Verb – prepositional phrase object.
segments.s670-g389: بپاسخ is an adverbial/prepositional compound meaning 'in reply' or 'to answer.' گشادند is a past/plural verbal form 'they opened' (root گشاد with plural ending -ند) and یکسر modifies the verb meaning 'entirely/at once.' زبان here stands for 'speech/utterance' (metonymic use of 'tongues'), so the literal order 'in reply opened-at‑once their tongues' yields 'their tongues opened at once in answer.'
segments.s670-g390: که‌ای is a poetic vocative/relative combination: که (that/who) plus the vocative ای (here contracted) used to address someone directly — effectively 'O ... who are'. The following adjectives نامور 'renowned' and پرهنر 'full of skill' modify پهلوان 'champion/hero', forming the direct address 'O famed, skilled champion.'
segments.s670-g391: چو here functions as a subordinating particle 'since/now that' (poetic usage), introducing the reason clause. ارج تو اینست 'your honour is this/so' is a copular clause (ارج = honour, تو = you, اینست = 'is this/so'), and نزدیک شاه 'near the king' is a predicative/locative phrase explaining the state: 'Now that your honour is thus near the king.'
segments.s670-g392: سگانند is a predicative statement 'are dogs' (سگ + -اند plural copula) used figuratively. بر بارگاهش is a prepositional phrase 'before/on his court' (بر + بارگاه + possessive -ش), and سپاه 'the host/army' appears at the end as the grammatical subject — a poetic inversion that yields 'the host are dogs before his court.'
segments.s670-g273: به یزدان here is an invocation/adjuration 'by God' (به used in the sense 'by'). که introduces a relative clause 'who ...' with او (third‑person pronoun) as its subject; برتر ازبرتریست is a predicative phrase meaning 'is higher than (all) superiority' where ازبرتریست is an archaic copular predicate 'is superior'. Word order is emphatic/poetic: the invocative preposition + divine name, then the relative clause.
segments.s670-g274: This is a simple nominal sentence with the copula attached to the final word: نگارنده (the writer/author) is the subject; زهره و مشتریست joins two nominal predicates with و ('and') and the copula -ست attached to مشتری (Jupiter). In context the line states a poetic attribution of the writer to the planets Venus and Jupiter.
segments.s670-g275: که چون introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'for/when'. بازگردی is 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive 'you return'; نپیچی is a negative present/subjunctive/imperative 'do not turn' (n‑ + present stem), with ز 'from' and من 'me' as its object. The clause is addressed to 'you' (implied subject) and orders not to turn away from the speaker.
segments.s670-g276: نه negates the following prepositional phrase: نه از ... = 'not from/of ...'. از is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; نامداران این انجمن is a genitival phrase 'the renowned ones of this assembly', with این pointing to the present gathering. Word order places the negation before the prepositional phrase for emphasis.
segments.s670-g277: بگفتند is simple past 'they said'. وز here is an archaic fusion of و + ز (and + from) used poetically: 'and from'. جای 'place/seat' is the object of ز, and برخاستند is past plural 'they rose' or 'they stood up', so the clause means 'they said and rose from their places.'
segments.s670-g278: سوی خوابگه is a directional phrase 'toward the sleeping‑place(s)'. رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' and functions as the object/complement of آراستند (past plural 'they made ready/arranged'), so the construction means 'they prepared to go toward the sleeping‑places.' The verb آراستند governs the infinitive, a common Persian periphrastic pattern.
segments.s670-g279: چو (fused here as چوبرزد) = 'when' + برزد (past tense 'struck/rose up'), used poetically to mark a temporal clause. سرازکوه is the phrase 'head from the mountain / rising from the mountain' (word order compressed), followed by the adjective زرد 'yellow' and noun آفتاب 'sun' — poetic inversion places the verb and prepositional phrase before the descriptive noun phrase.
segments.s670-g280: سرتاجداران is the plural subject 'the crowned ones / chief courtiers'; برآمد is the past singular/plural 'arose/rose up' (here plural meaning 'they rose'); زخواب is a fused preposition+noun 'from sleep' (ز = from + خواب = sleep). Together they state that the crowned rulers awoke/rose from sleep.
segments.s670-g409: بگفتند is past 3rd person plural of گفتن with the poetic prefix بـ, literally 'they said' (or 'it was said' depending on voice). وز پیش functions as a short adverbial phrase 'from (that) presence / from before (the court)' and بیرون شد(ند) means 'went out' — شد(ند) is the past plural of شدن used here as the main past verb 'went out/left'. The sequence gives: they were told/they said, and then from the presence they went out.
segments.s670-g410: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; کاخ همایون is a noun phrase 'the auspicious/imperial palace' (همایون qualifying کاخ). به + هامون marks motion 'to/into the plain'; شد(ند) again is the past plural of شدن marking that they went/arrived there.
segments.s670-g411: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (commander). سپه را uses را as the definite/object marker marking سپه 'the army' as the direct object. همی‌داد is an imperfective/continuous past form ('kept giving' — همی- signals ongoing action) and پند is the object 'advice/admonition'.
segments.s670-g412: همی‌داشت is another imperfective past 'kept/had' (here meaning he repeatedly held or uttered), با پند 'with advice' (با = 'with/by means of'), and then لب را ببند is an imperative clause: لب را ('the lip' with object marker) + ببند 'close!' — i.e. he kept saying by way of counsel: 'shut your lips.'
segments.s670-g413: چنین 'thus/so' sets the manner. تا + دوهفته expresses duration 'for two weeks' (تا = 'until/for' here). برین is composed of بر ('on/upon') + the demonstrative-like ین (here functioning as 'this/there' = 'in that place'), and برگذشت is past 'passed by/elapsed' — together: 'thus two weeks passed there.'
segments.s670-g414: سپهبد again the commander. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', ایوان is the porch/iwan they left, بیامد is past 'came' (3rd sing./pl. as narrative), and به دشت marks the destination 'to/into the plain.'
segments.s670-g415: یکی here means 'a certain' / 'one' (introducing something seen). بیشه 'grove/thicket' is the noun; پیش آمدش is the verb phrase 'came before him' (پیش = 'in front of/before', آمدش = 'came to him'); پر درخت means 'full of trees' (پر = 'full (of)', درخت = 'trees').
segments.s670-g416: This is a short adjectival/predicative tag: سزاوار 'suitable/fit for', میخواره 'wine‑drinker' (lit. 'one who drinks wine' — here nominative/description), نیکبخت 'fortunate/blessed' modifying the drinker. Together: 'fit for a fortunate wine‑drinker.'
segments.s670-g217: آغاز clause giving the source and result: 'از' (preposition) introduces the source 'from what was brought'. 'آورده' is a past participle/relative idea 'what was brought'. 'صد گنج شد ساخته' uses شد as an auxiliary ('was') plus ساخته ('made, fashioned') to express a past passive/result — literally 'a hundred treasures were made'. Word order is poetic (amount 'صد گنج' before the verbal phrase).
segments.s670-g218: Main clause about the king's feeling: 'دل شاه' (the king's heart) is the subject. 'زان کار' = 'because of/by that deed' (zira/زان + noun), and 'پرداخته' is a past participle used poetically to indicate the heart being affected/engaged by the action — here translated 'was moved/engaged'. The participial usage makes the clause terse and characteristic of classical Persian.
segments.s670-g219: Nominal phrase + verb: 'یکی' is the indefinite 'one/a certain'; 'تخت جامه' is a compound 'throne-cloth/robe for the throne' (noun + noun), and 'بفرمود' is the honorific past 'he commanded/ordered' with 'شاه' (the king) as the subject placed after the verb (typical poetic inversion).
segments.s670-g220: Relative/adverbial clause introduced by 'کز' (from/which): 'کز آنجا بیارند پیش سپاه' means 'which they are to bring from there before the army/host'. 'آن جا' = 'there', 'پیش سپاه' = 'in the presence of/the front of the army'. Note: 'بیارند' here functions as a subjunctive/imperative 'they bring' (classical form).
segments.s670-g221: 'همان' is the demonstrative 'that/same' placed before the phrase. 'بر کمر' is the prepositional phrase 'on the waist/belt' (preposition before noun). 'گوهر شاهوار' is a noun ('gem') followed by a qualifying adjective 'شاهوار' ('regal/lofty'), a common poetic order.
segments.s670-g222: A relative clause introduced by 'که' = 'which/that'. 'نامد' here means 'was named/called' (past form). 'همی' is an emphatic/continuous particle ('indeed/still') modifying the clause, and 'ارز او در شمار' = 'its worth/value was in the count' (literal: 'value of it in the tally'), i.e. its worth was reckoned among (the counted treasures). Word order places the predicate elements after the relative 'که', typical of classical style.
segments.s670-g223: 'یکی آفرین خاست' is a compact clause: 'یکی' ('one' or 'a certain') specifies a single cry; 'آفرین' is the acclamation 'bravo/acclamation'; 'خاست' (archaic past of خاستن) means 'arose/was raised'. 'از بزمگاه' is the source phrase 'from the banquet-hall'. The word order (verb after acclamation) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s670-g224: Reported wish/intention introduced by 'که' ('that'): 'پیروز باداین جهاندار شاه' is optative — 'پیروز بادا این جهاندار شاه' condensed to 'پیروز باداین ...' meaning 'may this sovereign king be victorious.' 'باد' (optative 'may be') is fused with 'این' here in classical poetic style; 'جهاندار شاه' is a lofty title 'sovereign king'.
segments.s670-g57: ولیکن is a coordinating adversative conjunction meaning 'but'. چو introduces a temporal clause here and = 'when'. در جنگ is a locative phrase placed before the noun (literally 'in war'). خواری is the noun 'humiliation/abasement' and بود is the past copula 'was'; the clause reads literally 'but when in-war humiliation was.'
segments.s670-g58: گه is an adverb meaning 'sometimes' or 'occasionally'. آشتی and بردباری are two abstract nouns placed together (effectively 'reconciliation (and) forbearance'); بود is again the past copula 'there was'. The structure omits a conjunction between the two nouns (understood as 'and').
segments.s670-g59: تو here functions as a dative/experiencer ('to/for you'); راخشم in the manuscript appears fused but functions as the noun 'anger' tied to/to‑you (the annotator's segmentation is nonstandard). با introduces the companion 'with' (با آشتی‌گر = 'with the peacemaker'), and یکیست is the predicative 'is one' (archaic single-word copular form meaning 'is the same'). Overall: 'For you, anger is the same as the reconciler/peacemaker.'
segments.s670-g60: خرد is the subject 'wisdom'. بی‌گمان is an adverbial modifier 'undoubtedly / doubtless'. نزد here marks relation or standpoint ('in the view/near to'); تواندکیست is a fused interrogative/relative verbal compound used here as a predicate meaning roughly 'knows/can tell who is able' or 'who can'. The whole clause: 'Wisdom, doubtless, knows who can (do it / be near).' Note the terse poetic fusion of auxiliary and interrogative.
segments.s670-g61: چو = 'when'. سالار is the noun 'commander / leader'. راه خداوند خویش is a genitival phrase 'the path of his lord' but here the annotator split خویش into خوی + ش; syntactically راه (way) + خداوند (lord) + خویش (his) yields 'the lordly way of his own master'.
segments.s670-g62: بگیرد is a present/subjunctive-type verbal form 'takes / should take' used in a general temporal clause ('when ... takes'). نیفتد is a negative present/subjunctive meaning 'does not fall / does not fail'. بهرکار ('for the task' / 'in the work') and پیش ('before / in the doing') complete the sense: 'he does not fail in the work before him' → 'he will not fail to do what is required.'
segments.s670-g63: همان = 'that very / the same'. راه = 'path/way' and یزدان = 'the Divine / God' (the indirect object/beneficiary). بباید + verb (here سپرد) forms a necessity/obligation ('must / should be entrusted'). Word order is poetic: indirect object (یزدان) appears before the infinitival verb سپرد (to entrust).
segments.s670-g64: ز = 'from' (poetic prepositional form). دل تیرگیها = 'the heart's dark things / dark spots' (تیرگیها plural 'darknesses' modifying دل). بباید + سترد again expresses necessity 'must be ...'; here the verb سترد is used in the sense 'be removed/wiped away' in context (poetic).
segments.s670-g209: بفرمود is a past, honorific/periphrastic verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تابار here functions as a verbal/nominalized phrase meaning 'to have/load a load' (an older compound), followed by آن اشتران 'those camels' as its object; the sequence Verb + (verbal noun) + object is a poetic ordering rather than modern colloquial syntax.
segments.s670-g210: بپشت is a contracted locative (بهِ پشت) meaning 'onto the back(s)'; اندر is a locative particle 'in/on/within' reinforcing location; آرند is a present/stative-plural form of آوردن ('they bring/are to bring') used here in a jussive/obligative sense 'bring (them)'; پیش سران is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the chiefs' (پیش = before/in front of + سران = leaders). Word order places the destination/location before the verb in this poetic register.
segments.s670-g211: کسی is an indefinite subject 'someone'; برگرفت is a past-tense transitive verb 'took up' or 'assumed' (here 'took on the counting'); از کشیدن شمار is a verbal-noun construction functioning as the object/complement — 'from/of drawing up the tally' (از + کشیدنِ شمار = 'the act of counting/listing'). The order Subject + Verb + از + verbal noun is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s670-g212: بیک روز is an adverbial temporal phrase meaning 'for one day' (here written as an older fused form), مزدور is the noun 'mercenary/paid man', and بدصدهزار is a numeral-amount 'to the amount of one hundred thousand'—together giving the amount paid per day. The clause omits an explicit subject ('he gave') because it's linked to the previous verb contextually.
segments.s670-g213: دگر روز هم introduces a new temporal clause 'another/one more day'; بامداد پگاه are two synonymous time adverbs 'at dawn/early morning' used together for emphasis (typical poetic doubling). Together they set the time frame before the action verb that follows.
segments.s670-g214: بخوان here is the fused prepositional/locative phrase (به خوان) meaning 'to the table/banquet/presence' (in older spellings it appears like a verbal form); برمی‌آورد is a present/future‑oriented compound verb 'he brings out/causes to be brought' and وبنشست is a past tense 'and he sat down' (و + بنشست). شاه is the subject of وبنشست placed after the verb for emphasis — a typical classical inversion.
segments.s670-g215: زمیدان (زِ میدان) is a fused prepositional phrase 'from the field/battlefield'; ببردند is a past plural verb 'they took' or 'they carried off'; پنجه هزار here is the poetic/orthographic rendering of a numeral phrase (пنج هزار / پنجه هزار) meaning 'fifty thousand' — numerals often appear in older orthography. The clause is a simple past narrative: from the field they took fifty thousand.
segments.s670-g216: هم 'also' links this clause to the previous; ازتنگ (از تنگ) means 'from the narrow pass / from distress' serving as another source; بر پشت مردان کار is a prepositional phrase 'upon the backs of the working men' (بر + پشت + مردانِ کار), indicating that the burdens were laid on those men's backs. The whole segment describes additional levies placed upon laboring men.
segments.s670-g265: "بدو گفت" places the indirect object (بدو, 'to him') before the verb گفت (past tense 'said'), a common older/poetic Persian word order. "سوگند ما" is the object phrase 'our oath', and تازه کن is an imperative compound (تازه 'fresh/new' + کن 'do' imperative) meaning 'make (it) fresh' or 'renew (it)'.
segments.s670-g266: همان کار is 'the same deed/work' (noun phrase). بر دیگر here introduces the manner/alternative ('upon/with another' or 'in another way'), and اندازه کن is an imperative 'measure/do (measure it)' (اندازه 'measure' + کن 'do' imperative). The whole segment gives an instruction to repeat the same action but change its measure/mode.
segments.s670-g267: بخوردند is a past-tense verb (3rd plural) introducing the action 'they took/received' in the classical/poetic phrasing; it governs the object سوگندهای گران ('weighty/solemn oaths'), where گران qualifies سوگندهای as 'heavy/solemn'.
segments.s670-g268: This is a sequence of prepositional oath-phrases introduced by به, here functioning as 'by' (formulaic oath). یزدان پاک = 'the pure God'; وبه (and‑by) links the next oath, جان سران = 'the lives/souls of the chiefs' (سران 'leaders/chiefs').
segments.s670-g269: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/which'. "از شاه خاقان" is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the king Khāqān' (از indicating source or relation). نپیچد is the negative present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن ('not turn/twist' or 'not be turned'), and به دل is the locative phrase 'into the heart' — together expressing 'that it does not turn into the king's heart' (i.e., does not vex/occur to the king).
segments.s670-g270: ندارد is third-person present 'he does not have / he has not'. به کاری is an idiomatic complement meaning 'a taste/penchant for (such) action' (به + کاری). ورا is a clitic form meaning 'toward him/them' or 'to him', and دلگسل is an adjective 'heart‑breaking / one who breaks hearts'. Together the clause says he lacks inclination for such acts and is not a heart‑breaker.
segments.s670-g271: This is a string of oath terms. بگاه 'by the hour/moment' and وبتاج 'and the crown' (the poetic spelling joins the conjunction with the noun). بخورشیدوماه is a fused prepositional phrase 'by the sun and moon' (ب- used instead of full به in poetic diction). The sequence is a conventional litany of witnesses invoked in oaths.
segments.s670-g272: A final pair of oath-witness names introduced by به 'by' (here again functioning as 'by' in the oath). آذرگشسب and آذرپناه are proper names (epic-personal/place names) invoked as guarantors; the repeated به ... و به ... structure is typical in formal oaths.
segments.s670-g137: که introduces a relative clause meaning “that/which.” The core clause is Subject–Object–IndirectObject–Verb: کیخسرو (subject) آن را (direct object; آن + object marker را fused as رابه in next token) به لهراسب (indirect object) داد (simple past “gave”). Note رابه here is the combination of را + به (’the/it’ + ’to’), marking the thing given and the recipient.
segments.s670-g138: که again begins a clause “that.” لهراسب is the subject; زان پس (= ز + آن + پس) is a two-word adverbial “after that/thereafter” (زان = ‘from that’, پس = ‘after’); بگشتاسب is the fused form of به گشتاسب (‘to Goshtasp’) and داد is the past tense verb “gave.” Word order is Subject – Adverbial – IndirectObject – Verb, with the preposition به attached to the name.
segments.s670-g139: که introduces this subordinate clause “that.” ارجاسب is the subject (a Turanian king); آن را is the definite direct object (آن + را); بدز is a verb root meaning ‘steal’ (here: ‘stole’), followed by درنهاد (past of در نهادن) meaning ‘placed into/put away.’ The two-verb sequence conveys ‘stole it and put it away.’
segments.s670-g140: که begins another clause giving the consequence: هنگام = ‘at the time’ + آنکس = ‘that person/people’ (here meaning ‘anyone’). ندارد is the present negative of داشتن ‘to have’ (i.e. ‘does not have’), and بیاد is an adverbial phrase ‘in memory/recall.’ The phrase آنکس ندارد بیاد means ‘no one has it in memory’ or ‘no one remembers it’ (negative subject + verb at the end: standard Persian word order with verb final).
segments.s670-g141: شمارش (‘the counting/number’) is fronted as the theme; ندانست is past negative ‘did not know’ or ‘knew not’; کس with a negative verb here means ‘no one.’ در جهان is a locative phrase ‘in the world.’ The order (object/theme – verb – subject/agent) is a poetic/topical inversion: literally ‘the counting knew-not anyone in the world’ → ‘no one in the world knew the count.’
segments.s670-g142: This is a nominative apposition listing the people who could not reckon it: ستاره‌شناسان is a compound noun (ستاره + شناسان) meaning ‘star‑knowers’ i.e. astronomers/astrologers; و = ‘and’; فرخ مهان is a compound epithet meaning ‘fortunate/auspicious great ones’ (a phrase for illustrious nobles). The two groups are simply coordinated as those who failed to reckon the number.
segments.s670-g143: نبشتند is the past plural ‘they wrote/inscribed’ (classical form of نوشتند). یک‌یک is an adverb ‘one by one’ placed after the verb to emphasize enumeration; همه خواسته is the direct object ‘all the wished/desired things’ (خواسته can be read as ‘wishes’ or ‘things requested’). Subject is omitted but understood (‘they’ — the experts or scribes).
segments.s670-g144: که starts a relative clause modifying خواسته: بود is the past copula ‘was’; اندر = ‘in/within’; آن گنج = ‘that treasure’; آراسته is an adjective/past-participial used predicatively ‘adorned’ or ‘well-arranged.’ Together: ‘that were in that adorned treasure.’ Note Persian places the copula before the locative phrase and the adjective after the noun phrase.
segments.s670-g9: بیامد is the past tense verb 'came' (subject omitted, third person). بنزد is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to the presence of / up to'. دبیر بزرگ is a noun + postpositive adjective ('the great scribe'); Persian often places the adjective after the noun. The whole segment thus means 'he came up to the great scribe.'
segments.s670-g10: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him/unto him' marking the indirect object. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' introducing the following clause (here it introduces reported speech); the provided sense list did not contain the verbal sense so it is flagged accordingly. کین here functions as a demonstrative 'this' (archaic use) introducing the following noun phrase. پهلوان سترگ is noun + adjective 'mighty/great champion' (adjective after noun). The clause reads 'he said to him, "this champion is mighty."'
segments.s670-g11: This segment uses an emphatic/archaic quantifier + measure phrase: بیک here functions like 'not even one / a single' (archaic/contracted), followed by پر پشه 'a fly's feather' as a minimal measure. ندارد is the present-tense negative 'does not have,' and خرد is the direct object 'wisdom.' Together: 'He does not have even a fly's feather of wisdom.'
segments.s670-g12: ازیرا is an archaic/contracted connective meaning 'for this reason / because of this' (literally 'from this'); کسی را is the object phrase 'anyone' with the Persian object marker را. بکس نشمرد is a negative past-tense construction: نشمرد (he did not reckon/consider) with بکس (here functioning idiomatically as 'any person / to anyone') — the combined sense is 'he did not count/consider anyone (on that account).'
segments.s670-g13: ببایدش is a modal/impersonal construction 'it must (be) to him' where the enclitic -ش (= 'to him') attaches to بباید; گفتن is the infinitive 'to say.' کزین = 'from this / because of this'; چاره نیست = 'there is no remedy.' Grammatically this whole clause means 'it must be told to him that there is no remedy for this.'
segments.s670-g14: ورا = 'to him' (object/clitic), بتر از = 'worse than,' خشم = 'anger,' پتیاره is an archaic/uncertain noun in the text (left untranslated or treated as a name/term), and نیست = 'is not / there is not.' The phrase reads 'for him there is nothing worse than the anger of Patiyareh' (i.e. 'nothing is worse for him than Patiyareh's anger').
segments.s670-g15: به نزدیک بهرام is a prepositional phrase 'to/near Bahram.' رفت is the past tense verb 'went.' آن دو مرد is the subject 'the two men' placed after the verb (typical flexible Persian word order). So: 'The two men went near Bahram.'
segments.s670-g16: زبانها is the plural 'tongues/speeches' (i.e. their speech), پراز is the prepositional adjective 'full of,' بند here is used in the poetic sense 'lines/stanzas' or rhetorical tropes, و = 'and,' رخ = 'face,' لاژورد = 'lapis-lazuli' (a poetic color image). The image reads 'their tongues full of verse/stanza and their faces lapis-blue,' i.e. they spoke in measured poetic lines and had bright, blue-like faces (a poetic epithet).
segments.s670-g345: Subject + circumstantial phrase + verb + adverb: فرستاده (the envoy) is the subject, با خلعت is a prepositional phrase 'with the robe' attached to the subject, آمد is the past tense verb 'came', and چوباد (an old/poetic fusion of چو باد) is an adverbial 'like the wind/quickly' placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s670-g346: شنیده is a perfect/participial form 'having heard' that sets up a circumstantial clause; سخنها (the speeches/words) is its object, همه 'all' quantifies سخنها, and کردن with یاد (کرد یاد) is a light-verb construction meaning 'to remember' (literally 'made remembrance').
segments.s670-g347: چو is the conjunction 'when'; the clause has the subject بهرام, followed by the instrument/associate phrase با نامه خلعت ('with the letter [and] the robe' or 'the robe accompanied by a letter'), and بدید is the past tense 'saw' (poetic older form of دید). Word order here is typical of narrative: temporal conjunction + subject + object/phrase + verb.
segments.s670-g348: شکیبایی و خامشی (patience and silence) are the coordinated direct objects (virtues) and برگزید is the past transitive verb 'chose/selected'; the subject (Bahrām) is carried over from the previous clause, so the verb appears without a separate subject noun.
segments.s670-g349: همی‌گفت is an iterative/imperfective past 'kept saying' or 'would say' (poetic compound). کینست is a contracted poetic form (کین است) meaning 'it is revenge' (predicate), and پاداش من ('my reward/recompense') stands in apposition to that predicate — together: 'he kept saying, "This is my recompense (i.e. revenge)."'
segments.s670-g350: چنین 'thus/so' links to what he said; the prepositional-like phrase از پی here means 'because of / as a result of' (از = because of, پی = after/following), and شاه پرخاش من is a genitival phrase 'the king's reproach of me' or more naturally 'my reproach of the king' depending on emphasis — overall: 'such is the recompense for my reproach of the king.'
segments.s670-g351: چنین بد 'such badness' is the subject phrase; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', so ز اندیشه شاه means 'from the king's thought/intention' (اندیشه used here as 'intention'), and نیست is the negative copula 'is not' — the clause denies that the king harbors such ill intent.
segments.s670-g352: جز 'except' introduces the sole exception; ار is the conditional 'if', ناسزا 'abusive words/insults', گفت would normally be the past verb 'said' here but the provided sense list does not include that reading (see note), and بدخواه نیست 'is not an ill-wisher' negates that someone is malevolent — together: 'except if (one) uttered insults, (then) he is not an ill-wisher.'
segments.s670-g305: چهارم here marks ordinal/iteration (“for the fourth time”). فرستاد is a past-tense verb meaning ‘sent/dispatched’ and takes خاقان as its subject (the Khāqān did the sending). کسی is the indefinite direct object 'someone' (the person sent). Word order is Subject–Verb–Object in a compressed epic style.
segments.s670-g306: که introduces a relative/reported-speech clause modifying کسی ('someone who ...'). برگرد is an imperative/jussive meaning 'return!' (reported order). چون here functions as 'because/for' or 'when' linking the reason or circumstance. رنج means 'suffering/hardship' and دیدی is second-person past 'you saw' (reported wording of the envoy). بسی is an adverbial 'much/very' (intensifier). The whole clause quotes what the messenger told him.
segments.s670-g307: چو بشنید (here written چوبشنید) is a fused temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو + شنید). بهرام is the subject (proper name). برگشت is a past verb 'returned/withdrew/turned back.' از is the preposition 'from/away from' and وی is the third-person pronoun 'him/that one' or 'that matter' — together 'برگشت از وی' = 'he withdrew from him/that.'
segments.s670-g308: بتندی is an aorist/past-form meaning 'he hastened' (بـ- prefix + تندی/tendency to speed). سوی is the preposition 'toward.' بلخ is the place-name 'Balkh.' بنهاد is a past verb 'he put/placed/turned' (here 'set/turned his face'); روی is 'face' used idiomatically with بنهاد to mean 'he set his face toward' (= he headed for).
segments.s670-g309: همی‌بود is an imperfect/continuous past idiom 'continued to be' or 'was remaining.' دربلخ is the locative 'in Balkh' (در + بلخ). چندی here means 'for a while' (a temporal adverb). دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' describing his mood. Word order places the temporal and locative phrases before the predicate, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s670-g310: زکرده is a causative/ablative phrase 'from/for what [he] had done' (ز + کرده). پشیمان is the predicate adjective 'regretful.' و‌دل links with 'and his heart' (ودل = و + دل). پر is a predicative adjective 'full (of)' and زغم gives the content 'of grief/sorrow.' Together: 'regretful for what he had done and with a heart full of sorrow.'
segments.s670-g311: جهاندار is the title 'the sovereign/ruler' (subject). ز is the preposition 'from/of' introducing the target of displeasure (زو = ز + او = 'from him' / 'with him'). هم is 'also/too' and نه negates خشنودبود (خشنود بود = 'was pleased'); the negative construction 'نه خشنودبود' gives 'was not pleased.' The overall sense: 'The ruler, too, was not pleased with him.'
segments.s670-g312: زتیزی is an adverbial/phrastic form meaning 'with swift anger/irritation' (ز + تیزی; here 'swiftly' or 'sharply' modifying the state). روانش ('his soul/spirit') is the possessed noun. پراز is an alternative form of پرَ از 'full of.' دود is literal 'smoke' used figuratively, and بود is the past copula 'was.' Together: 'his spirit was swiftly aflame/angry and full of smoke' (a poetic image of inner turmoil).
segments.s670-g25: هم (an emphatic/temporal particle) + اندر زمان شد is a literary sequence meaning 'it came to pass at that time'; شد is the past/telic verb 'happened/was' and governs the following directional phrase به نزدیک اوی, where به + نزدیک expresses motion toward and اوی (old poetic form of او) is the third‑person object/goal 'him'. Word order is archaic/literary: temporal adverb → verbless clause 'came to pass' → directional complement.
segments.s670-g26: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'); روشن is an adjective used as the complement of کند (make/bring about) meaning 'to enlighten/make bright'; کند is the present tense (or historical present used in narration) 'does/makes'; جان تاریک اوی is the object phrase 'his dark soul' with اوی as genitival/possessive — the whole clause = 'so that he might make his dark soul bright.'
segments.s670-g27: همی‌بود is a durative/continuous past form 'kept being / continued' (literary); تا is the temporal/subordinating conjunction 'until'; او is the subject 'he'; میان را is the definite object 'the middle/space' marked by را. The clause means 'he continued until he (did) the closing of the intervening space.'
segments.s670-g28: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase 'at one time / suddenly' (one + occasion); تیزتگ is a poetic/archaic noun meaning 'sharp blade/dagger' (the instrument or image of a sudden attack); برنشست is the past of برنشستن literally 'rose up upon / alighted on' (here used figuratively of a blade/strike). The sequence describes a sudden, sudden-onset action.
segments.s670-g29: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed (general)'; همی‌راند is a habitual/continuous past 'was driving/urging on'; با + اوبه is a compacted prepositional phrase (اوبه = او + به) indicating accompaniment or direction 'with him/to him'; راه is the noun 'road/path' object or locative complement. Together: 'the spahbed kept driving him on the road.'
segments.s670-g30: بدید is the past 'he saw'; آنک introduces a nominal content clause 'that/which'; تازه نبد is a negative past construction ('no fresh/new one had been' — نبد = 'was not / did not become'); روی شاه is the noun phrase 'the face/presence before the king' (روی = face/surface, شاه = king). The clause reports that he observed that a fresh face/appearance had not yet been presented to the king.
segments.s670-g31: بهنگام is a prepositional/time adverb 'at the time of'; پدرود کردنش is a verbal noun + object/possessive: پدرود 'farewell' + کردن 'to do' with the enclitic ش 'his/her', so 'his taking of leave'; گفت here marks the speech act 'he said' (in this lexical list گفت is glossed as 'conversation/discussion' but in context functions as the verb 'said'). The whole segment sets the scene 'as he was saying farewell.'
segments.s670-g32: که introduces the reported speech/clause; آزار داری is a present/optative clause 'you have (any) harm/grievance' where داری is the present second‑person 'you have'; ز من is the prepositional phrase 'from me / against me'; درنهفت is a negative imperative or hortatory form 'do not hide' (دَر + نهفت, with نهفت from نهفتن 'to hide'). Grammatically this is a request/command embedded under گفت: 'he said that, if you have harm from me, do not hide it.'
segments.s670-g377: زلشکر is an opaque/rare noun here (likely a name or obscure term) acting as the subject. برفتند is past tense, 3rd-person plural (بر + رفتند) 'they went/approached'. نزدیک + اوی is a predicative/adverbial phrase 'near him' — اوی is an older/poetic 3rd-person pronoun/enclitic meaning 'him/he'. Word order is subject – verb – adverbial.
segments.s670-g378: This is a string of adjectival/predicative phrases describing the same person: پراندیشه (پر- prefix + اندیشه) 'full of thought / pensive'; بد جان literally 'bad/ill-souled' (بد modifying جان); تاریک اوی places the adjective before the pronoun for emphasis, 'dark was his soul' (تاریک = gloomy/dark, اوی = his/he). The phrases are coordinate/descriptive and relate back to the person referred to by اوی in the previous segment.
segments.s670-g379: چورفتند is چو ('when') fused to رفتند 'they went' yielding a temporal clause 'when they went'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and/then'. دیدند is past tense 'they saw'. پیر وجوان is a simple noun phrase 'an old man and a youth' (two coordinated nouns).
segments.s670-g380: بران گونه is the idiom بر آن گونه 'in that manner / of that sort' (بر + آن + گونه); the order is somewhat poetic. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. پوشش پهلوان is a noun phrase 'the champion's covering/garment' (پوشش = covering/cloak; پهلوان specifies whose it is). The whole segment characterizes the appearance/garb observed.
segments.s670-g381: بماندند is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they stayed / remained'. زان کار = از آن کار 'from/at that affair' (زان = زِ آن = 'from that' or 'about that'). یکسر شگفت is an adverbial phrase 'entirely amazed' or 'completely in wonder' (یکسر = wholly; شگفت = wonder/astonishment). The clause says they stayed there, wholly astonished at that event.
segments.s670-g382: دل هرکس اندیشه‌ای برگرفت is an SOV-style clause: 'a thought (اندیشه‌ای) seized (برگرفت) the heart (دل) of each person (هرکس)'. اندیشه‌ای uses the -ای suffix to indicate 'a/an thought' (indefinite). برگرفت is past tense 'took up/seized' (here used intransitively in the sense 'a thought took hold of each heart').
segments.s670-g383: چنین 'thus/so' opens the reported speech. گفت here is the past tense verb 'said' (the lemma list provided is nominal but the form is verbal). پس 'then/after' places the timing; پهلوان 'the champion' is the speaker; با سپاه 'with the army' is an instrumental/associative phrase. Word order is topical: adverbial + verb + temporal + subject + accompaniment (poetic flexibility).
segments.s670-g384: که introduces the content of what was said ('that'). خلعت is the direct object 'robe of honour / cloak'. بدین سان is the idiom 'in this way' (here presented as b + دین + سان in the tokenization; syntactically it functions as a prepositional/adverbial phrase). فرستاد شاه = 'the king sent' (فرستاد, past tense 'sent', with شاه as subject). Note: one of the tokenized pieces (دین) does not match the semantic inventory here and functions as part of the compound بدین سان.
segments.s670-g129: همان نیز چیزی که کانی بود is a nominal clause: همان نیز (“that same, too”) is a demonstrative + adverb stressing continuity; چیزی که introduces a relative clause (“something that…”). کانی is used as a substantive adjective meaning “like a mine/ore,” and بود is the past copula marking a descriptive past state (“was”). Word order is demonstrative → adverb → head noun → relative marker → predicate.
segments.s670-g130: کجا رستنش آسمانی بود is a subordinate clause giving the origin/character of that “something.” کجا functions here as an adverb/relative sense “whence/where,” رستنش is the infinitive/stem رستن with the third‑person possessive suffix -ش (“its rising/being born/origin”), آسمانی is the predicate adjective “heavenly,” and بود is the past copula (“was”). The whole unit = “whose rising was heavenly.”
segments.s670-g131: همه گنجها اندر آورده بود is a perfect‑like past statement: همه گنجها (“all the treasures”) is the definite plural object/subject phrase, اندر آورده is a compound verbal participle/verb meaning “brought in / collected,” and بود is the past auxiliary giving past perfect sense (“had brought”). Note the inverted order where the participle precedes the auxiliary.
segments.s670-g132: کجا نام او در جهان برده بود is another relative/adverbial clause: کجا here = “where/whence” (linking to the previous clause), نام او (“his name”) is the topic (او = he), در جهان = “in the world,” and برده بود is the compound past of بردن used passively/causatively “had been carried/was borne” or more idiomatically “had been made known.” The clause states that his name was spread through the world.
segments.s670-g133: زچیز سیاوش نخستین کمر is a brief cataloging clause: زچیز (“from the things/of the possessions”) marks origin/possession, سیاوش names the owner, نخستین is a numeral/ordinal “first,” and کمر here means “girdle/belt.” The phrase lists the first item taken from Siyâvash’s goods.
segments.s670-g134: بهرمهره‌ای در سه یاره گهر continues the list: بهرمهره‌ای literally “for/as a مهره (bead/pendant)” (بهر indicates purpose/use), در is the locative/prepositional “in/among,” سه یاره reads as “three companions/attendants” (i.e., threefold or for three companions), and گهر is the noun “gem/jewel.” The unit describes further items — a pendant and a jewel associated with three companions/settings.
segments.s670-g135: همان گوشوارش که اندر جهان is a nominal phrase introducing the specific object: همان (“that same/that very”) emphasizes identity, گوشوارش is گوشوار + -ش = “his earring/ornament,” که is a relative connector “which/that,” and اندر جهان “in the world” sets the domain where the following claim holds.
segments.s670-g136: کسی را نبود ازکهان ومهان is a negative existential clause: کسی را نبود = “there was no one” (کسی here negative sense), ازکهان و مهان is a stock expression meaning “from the ancients and the great” (i.e., among those of old and of high rank). را is the object/topic marker attached for emphasis, and نبود is the past negative of بودن (“was not / there was not”).
segments.s670-g361: زشاه جهان is a prepositional/agent-like phrase: ز (ze) = 'by/from' attached to شاه جهان 'king of the world', so the phrase means 'by the king of the world'. اینچنین کارکرد is a classical/perfective verbal clause meaning 'did thus' or 'acted in this way' (کار + کرد). Word order places the actor-marking preposition before the title and the verb phrase after.
segments.s670-g362: نزیبد is an impersonal present/gnomic predicate with negative prefix ن- + زیبیدن/زیب meaning 'it is not fitting/it does not become'. به پیش = 'in the presence of / before' (به + پیش), and خردمند مرد is a noun phrase 'a/that wise man'. Together: 'it is not fitting before a wise man.'
segments.s670-g363: ازان پس که is a temporal connector: از آن پس = 'after that', followed by که introducing a subordinate clause ('when/that'). با introduces an instrumental phrase 'with', here + خار مایه سپاه is a noun cluster: خار (literally 'thorn', used metaphorically for something meagre or contemptible) + مایه 'resource/means' + سپاه 'army', so 'with the meagre resource of the army'.
segments.s670-g364: بتندی here functions as an adverbial/verb meaning 'quickly/with haste' (from تندیدن/tند). برفتم is an archaic past first-person form of برفتن/رفت‌ن 'to depart, to go away' ('I went/left'). زدرگاه شاه = ز + درگاه شاه 'from the king's court/threshold'. The clause therefore means 'I hastened away from the king's court.'
segments.s670-g365: همه دیده‌اند is a perfect plural 'all/everyone have seen'. آنچ is a relative/indefinite compound meaning 'that which / what', followed by من کرده‌ام 'I have done' (present perfect first person). The clause: 'All have seen what I have done.'
segments.s670-g366: غم و رنج و سختی is a noun series 'sorrow and anguish and hardship'. که introduces a relative clause 'that', and من برده‌ام is present-perfect first-person 'I have borne/carried' (بردن used in the sense of enduring). So: 'the sorrow and suffering and hardship that I have borne.'
segments.s670-g367: چوپاداش is a fused phrase: چو = 'as' + پاداش 'reward' giving 'as a reward' or 'the reward was'. آن رنج خواری = 'that suffering (and) abasement/humiliation'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause states: 'As a reward for that suffering there was humiliation.'
segments.s670-g368: گر is a conditional 'if'. ازبخت = 'from/by fate' (i.e., 'because of ill fortune') and ناسازگاری = 'discord/incompatibility' (here a quality of fate or circumstance). بود is again the past copula 'was'. The clause qualifies the previous line: 'if it was due to ill fortune/incompatibility.'
segments.s670-g169: چوبشنید is the fused classical form meaning 'when he heard' (چو + بشنید). شاه جهان is the title 'king of the world' with the noun (شاه) followed by an epithet (جهان). برنشست is a past/telic verbal form (بر + نشست) meaning 'took a seat / sat up / mounted' and completes the clause begun by the temporal 'چو...'.
segments.s670-g170: به سر بر یکی تاج و گرزی بدست is a compact nominal phrase describing the king's attributes: به سر (on the head) + بر یکی تاج (one crown placed upon) and گرزی بدست (a mace in [his] hand). Word order is locative (به سر) + postposed modifier (بر یکی تاج). بدست here is a frozen locative/possessive expression 'in the hand'.
segments.s670-g171: بیامد چنین تا بدرگه رسید is a sequence of actions: بیامد ('he came') + چنین ('thus/so') and the subordinating تا ('until') introducing the endpoint بدرگه رسید ('reached the court/threshold'). رسید is simple past/perfect 'arrived'.
segments.s670-g172: ز دهلیز چون روی خاقان بدید: ز دهلیز = 'from the corridor' (ز as poetic 'from'); چون + ... بدید here functions temporally ('when he... saw') introducing the following perception clause. روی خاقان is the direct object 'the face/appearance of the khaghan'; بدید is past 'saw'.
segments.s670-g173: همی‌بود تا چونش بیند به راه: همی‌بود is an imperfective/habitual past ('it was the custom' / 'it kept being so'), تا introduces the temporal boundary 'until/that when', and چونش بیند ('when he sees him') is a temporal subordinate clause; به راه ('on the road') gives the location of that seeing. The verb بیند is classical present/subjunctive form used narratively for a (habitual) action.
segments.s670-g174: فرود آید او همچنان با سپاه: فرود آید is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'he descends / comes down' (فرود = descent, آید = comes). او is the pronoun 'he'; همچنان ('likewise/in the same way') links his action to what precedes; با سپاه 'with the army' indicates accompaniment. The clause states that on seeing him he would dismount along with his troops.
segments.s670-g175: ببیندش و برگردد از پیش اوی: ببینَدش is a present/subjunctive or optative form meaning 'he sees him' / 'he might see him' and here narratively reports the observed action; the clitic -ش attaches as 'him'. و connects the verbs; برگردد is 'turn back/return'; از پیش اوی = 'from before him' (از = from, پیش اوی = in his presence/of him). The sequence expresses two successive actions (see, then turn away).
segments.s670-g176: پراندیشه بد زان سخن نامجوی is a short descriptive phrase: پراندیشه ('full of thought' = pensive/worried) modifies the person; بد is an adjectival adverb 'bad/uneasy' strengthening the negative mood; زان ('because of that / from that') links cause; سخن = 'speech/talk'; نامجوی here is an adjective/noun 'seeking a name/fame-seeking' (i.e., talk motivated by seeking renown). Together: 'troubled and ill because of that fame-seeking speech.'
segments.s670-g73: بدو (prepositional phrase) = 'to him / unto him' comes first for emphasis; گفت is the past verb 'said' (the speaker's action); خاقان is the subject 'the Khaghan' placed after the verb (poetic inversion). که begins the reported content (a subordinate clause) and هرشهریار is the indefinite subject 'every king / every sovereign' inside that clause.
segments.s670-g74: که introduces a relative/conditional clause 'who/that'; ازنیک و بد is a coordinated prepositional phrase 'from (or between) good and bad/evil'; برنگیرد is a negative present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'does not turn/does not change (away)' and governs the clause; شمار at the end functions like an imperative or verb 'count / reckon / consider' addressing the listener—its placement at the clause end is typical for commands or summative verbs in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s670-g75: ببد کردن is a light verb construction where ببد (here 'became/turned') plus کردن gives the sense 'to turn/become (bad)'; بنده is the noun 'servant' (likely speaker's self‑reference or an attendant); خامش 'silent' is a predicate adjective; بود is the past copula 'was/ remained'—together they mean 'the servant became silent' or 'the servant was silent when (he) turned bad.'
segments.s670-g76: برمن is 'as for me / toward me' marking the perspective; چنان دان is an idiomatic imperative/optative 'consider/think so' (چنان = 'so, thus'; دان = 'consider/know'); که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'; بیهش is an enclitic/old form meaning 'to/for him' and بود is the past copula 'was'—literally: 'Consider me so, as he was toward him.'
segments.s670-g77: چو از دور بیند is a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if he sees from afar' (چو/چَو = when/if; از دور = from a distance; بیند = sees). ورا is the object 'him' or 'toward him', and بدسگال is a predicate/description 'ill‑fated / ill‑omened' attached to that object. Word order places the sighting clause before the result/description.
segments.s670-g78: وگر introduces an alternative conditional 'and if / but if'; نیک here means 'good / well' and خواهی is a verbal noun/inflected form meaning 'wishing / desire' (so نیک‌خواهی = 'wishing good for him'); بود‌گر is a conditional/form meaning 'if you would be' or 'if it were so'; همال ('companion/porter' figuratively) is used as 'companion/ally'—together: 'and if you wish him well, be his companion.'
segments.s670-g79: تو را is the direct object 'you' with the object marker را; ناسزا خواند is a verbal phrase 'called (you) abusive names / insulted you' — خواند here is used in the sense 'called' (to call someone names); وسرسبک attaches an adjectival phrase 'and (was) light‑hearted / flippant' describing the manner.
segments.s670-g80: ورا is 'to him / at him' marking the target of the following epithets; شاه ایران is the noun phrase 'the King of Iran' (شاه + Iran); ومغزی تنگ is a compound adjective 'and of narrow mind / stingy of understanding' (مغزی = 'mind/brainy/pithy', تنگ = 'narrow') — together they report the names/epithets applied to that person.
segments.s670-g249: هم اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase meaning "also/at that time" (هم = "also/too" + اندر زمان = "in the time/then"). گفت is the verb 'said' in the past, but here appears in a poetic/elliptical usage introducing reported speech; چوبینه راه is a compound noun phrase with چوبینه (a wooden cudgel/staff) as head and راه (the road/way) specifying its location or role.
segments.s670-g250: همی is an adverb of continuity here meaning "continually/still"; گم کند uses کند as the present/habitual auxiliary (archaic poetic present) so the clause means "keeps getting lost." سربرآرد is a compound verb meaning "raises its head" and بماه (به + ماه fused) means "toward the moon"; together the clause describes a repeated action directed at the moon.
segments.s670-g251: یکی آنک is an introductory phrase meaning "one (a certain one) who/that" (آنک as the relative/pointing particle). خاقان is the subject/title 'Khaghan' and چین را بَزد is a past-tense verb phrase with را marking چین (China) as direct object: بَزد is the archaic past of زدن "to strike/attack."
segments.s670-g252: ازان سان که is a fixed idiomatic connector "in such a manner that/so that" (ازان سان = "from that manner" + که introducing a clause). ازگوهر بد سزد is an impersonal/gnomic statement: ازگوهر ("from/of a gem"), بد ("bad/unseemly") and سزد is the present impersonal/3rd-person form "it befits / it is fitting" — together: "which is such as to be unbecoming of a jewel."
segments.s670-g253: دگر آنک begins another relative clause: "another (one) who/that..."; چون here functions as a temporal conjunction "when/once" and گوشوارش (گوشوار + possessive ـش) is "his earring." به کار is the idiom "into use/into work" (به = to/toward, کار = use/function), so چون ... به کار بیامد = "when ... came into use."
segments.s670-g254: بیامد is the past of آمدن used here intransitively "came/appeared" ("came into being/into use"). مگرشد is an idiomatic past-formation meaning "so it happened/then it came about." یکی شهریار is a nominal phrase "a certain king" (یکی = a certain one, شهریار = king) serving as the result or outcome introduced by مگرشد.
segments.s670-g255: همه رنج او is the subject "all his suffering." سر به سر is a reduplicated idiom meaning "completely/altogether," placed before the predicate. بادگشت is a compound noun (باد + گشت) meaning "reversal/turning about," used predicatively to indicate the change that occurred to his suffering.
segments.s670-g256: همه داد دادنش is a noun phrase: همه داد = "all (his) justice" and دادنش (داد + نش, here a deverbal noun with the enclitic referring back) = "his giving (of justice)." بیداد گشت uses گشت (past of گشتن) as "became" so the clause reads "all his giving of justice became bīdād (injustice/oppression)."
segments.s670-g49: This is a reporting phrase: بدو (literally 'to him') marks the indirect object placed before the verb, followed by گفت which is the past tense 'said' (poetic word order places verb before subject). بهرام is the subject (the one who spoke). کای is a poetic vocative 'O' placed before the epithet نامجوی, which addresses the addressee as 'name-seeker' or 'seeker of renown.'
segments.s670-g50: سخنها is the plural object 'words/speeches' and چنین modifies it 'such/like this.' تا ... توانی is a concessive phrase 'as far as you can' (تا + توانی), where توانی expresses ability. مگوی is the negative prohibitive ('do not say' / second person singular negative imperative). The overall order places the prohibition at the end after the concessive phrase.
segments.s670-g51: چرا is the interrogative 'why' introducing a rhetorical question. The subject من ('I') and the indirect object بتو ('to you') frame the verb بیاراستم, here a past tense first-person form meaning 'I adorned/arranged' (poetic: 'I set/adorned my heart'). دل is the direct object 'heart.' Word order is emphatic/poetic (object and indirect object follow the subject).
segments.s670-g52: ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'from/for the world' (here meaning 'for the sake of the world' or 'in the world'). تو را uses را as the object marker with 'you' (marking the beneficiary/target). نیکویی is the noun 'goodness' or 'good fortune' desired for the addressee, and خواستم is the past first‑person 'I wished/desired.' The clause therefore states a motive or wish directed toward the addressee.
segments.s670-g53: ز تو here means 'because of you' / 'on your account' (poetic use of ز). نامه is the direct object 'a letter' and کردم is the past 'I made/wrote.' بشاه جهان is a dative phrase 'to the king of the world' (بشاه = 'to the king'); the phrase as a whole says the speaker composed/sent a letter concerning the addressee to the sovereign.
segments.s670-g54: همی is an adverb 'always/still' placed before the verb for emphasis. زشت تو is a noun phrase 'your ugliness/faults' (زشت used nominally), داشتم is the past 'I had/kept' (here 'I kept' or 'I held'), and در نهان is a locative phrase 'in secret' or 'hidden.' Together: 'I always kept your faults hidden.'
segments.s670-g55: بدو 'to him' again places the indirect object before the verb. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past). خاقان is the speaker ('the khāqān'). که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...', آن بد گذشت = 'that evil/bad thing has passed' where گذشت is the past 'passed/was gone.'
segments.s670-g56: گذشته modifies 'سخنها' as 'past words' (گذشته سخنها = 'words already said'). همه 'all' quantifies them. باد here is the optative/particle 'may' expressing a wish, and گشت is the verb 'became/be gone' — together forming a wish: 'may all past words be gone' or 'let all past words be past.'
segments.s670-g257: بگفت is a past‑tense narrative verb 'he said' (classical past). این و links what was just said ('this') with the next action using و ('and'). پرموده را پیش خواند is an object phrase + verb: پرموده را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker and پیش‌خواند (preposition + verb) means 'called/summoned forward' (پیش = 'to the presence/forward', خواند here = 'summoned/called'). The whole clause means 'he said this and called Pormudeh forward.'
segments.s670-g258: بران is a temporal/consequential adverb 'thereupon' (literally 'on that'), naming the consequence. نامور is an adjective 'renowned' describing the person who is the object. پیشگاهش is پیشگاه + enclitic ـش ('his/its') meaning 'in his presence' or 'before him/at court'. نشاند is an archaic/transitive past form meaning '(caused to) sit, seated' (i.e. 'had him seated').
segments.s670-g259: ببودند and خوردند are two coordinated past verbs in the third‑person plural: ببودند 'they were/they remained' and خوردند 'they ate'. تا شب sets the temporal limit 'until night'. زراه is an adverbial phrase 'زِ راه' meaning 'by/on the road' (literally 'from the way'), giving the location for the action.
segments.s670-g260: بیفشاند is a past/transitive verb (here narrative past) meaning 'he cast/let fall, scattered' (بی‑ prefix + افشاند). آن is the demonstrative 'that'. تیره زلف سیاه is a three‑word noun phrase 'that dark/black tress' (تیره = 'dark', زلف = 'lock/tress of hair', سیاه = 'black'), the direct object of بیفشاند.
segments.s670-g261: بخاقان چین functions as a nominal subject 'the Khaghan (ruler) of China' (historical title + place). گفت is the past‑tense verb 'said' introducing reported speech. کز بهر من begins a subordinate/explanatory clause: کز = 'that/for which' (introducing reason), بهر = 'for/on account of', من = 'me'. Together: 'the Khaghan of China said that for my sake...'.
segments.s670-g262: بسی modifies زنج and here functions as an intensifier 'many/much' (classical usage). زنج = 'chains' is the noun being quantified. دیدی is second‑person past 'you saw' (or 'you have seen' in natural rendering). توازشهرمن is a contracted phrase 'تو از شهر من' meaning 'you from my city' (= 'in my city' / 'in my town'); the order here places the pronoun تو before the prepositional/genitive phrase.
segments.s670-g263: نشسته is a participial/adjectival past form 'seated' describing the subject's posture. بیازید (imperf./aorist or classical narrative) here means 'opened (the mouth), gaped, broke forth'—used of starting to speak or showing emotion. ودستش گرفت coordinates with the previous verb: و + دستش (دست + enclitic ـش 'his/its') + گرفت 'he seized/took', so 'and he took his hand.'
segments.s670-g264: ازو ماند is a short clause 'from him/at him remained' — ازو = 'at/from him' and ماند is the past 'remained/was left'. پرموده is the same name/epithet as earlier ('the experienced/renowned one') and اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder' (اندر = 'in/within', شگفت = 'wonder/astonishment'). The clause as a whole: 'Pormudeh remained in wonder.'
segments.s670-g41: مرا (object) + بند (noun 'fetter') + گردون گردنده (a nominal phrase 'the turning/turning‑heavens') + کرد (past tense 'made/caused'). The object "مرا" is fronted for emphasis; "گردون گردنده" is a descriptive compound (گردون + participial adjective گردنده) meaning the revolving sky/fate. کرد is simple past 'made/caused'.
segments.s670-g42: نگویم is a negative first‑person clause ('I will not say' / 'let me not say') used as a modesty or restraint formula. که introduces the reported content; با من is a prepositional phrase 'to/with me'; بدی بنده کرد literally 'did badness and made (me) a servant' — بنده here means 'servant/slave' and کرد is 'made/caused'.
segments.s670-g43: ز گفتار = 'from/at the speech/words of'; اوگشت here reads as the archaic sequence او گشت ('he became') with او = 'he' and گشت = 'became/turned'. The verb cluster precedes the subject in this poetic order: the clause means 'from his words he became (something) — Bahram pale'.
segments.s670-g44: بپیچید is a past tense verb 'he turned/turned aside'; و simply links clauses; خشم is the noun 'anger' and از دلیری is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/of courage/bravery'; بخورد is used in the sense 'befell/seized' (an intransitive perfective sense 'happened to him'). The word order puts the verb before the agent phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s670-g45: چنین (thus) modifies the speech act; داد پاسخ is the past construction 'gave an answer' (داد = past 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces the content clause; آمد نشان = 'a sign came/appeared' with آمد in simple past.
segments.s670-g46: ز گفتار = 'from the words/speech of'; آن مهتر = 'that chief/headman' (آن = that, مهتر = chief); سرکشان is a plural noun modifying or in apposition to مهتر (literally 'the rebellious/insolent ones' here 'the rebels'), forming 'that chief of the rebels'. The whole phrase gives the source of the sign.
segments.s670-g47: که introduces reported speech/content. تخم بدی = 'the seed of evil' (تخم = 'seed', بدی = 'evil'); تا توانی = an adverbial phrase 'as much/as far as you can' (تا + توانی, subjunctive/ability form); مکار is an imperative/advisory adjective 'be crafty/deceitful' (here an exhortation: 'be deceitful to the extent you can').
segments.s670-g48: چوکاری (چو کاری) here means 'some sort of office/post' or 'some such thing' (poetic compound); برت = بر + ت (برت = 'upon you'); بر دهد روزگار = 'fate/fortune may give' with دهد = present/future‑oriented 'gives/may give' and روزگار = 'fate/fortune'. Together: 'so that fortune may give you some such post.'
segments.s670-g121: دبیران برفتند is a simple past clause: دبیران (the plural noun) is the subject and برفتند (third-person plural past) is the verb 'they departed/went'. دل پرهراس is a short nominative/adjunct phrase ('heart fearful') describing their state — دل (heart) + پرهراس (adjective 'fearful/terrified') placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s670-g122: This adverbial clause explains elapsed time/reason: ز here functions as 'because of' (poetic usage), شبگیر is 'night-visitor/night-raid' (the cause), and تاشب گذشته سه پاس literally 'three passes/watches passed until (the) night' — گذشته is a participle 'passed/gone' governing the numeral phrase سه پاس (three watches). Word order is causal/temporal: 'because of the night-visitor, three watches of the night passed.'
segments.s670-g123: This clause uses a poetic predicate: سیه شد ('became black/dark') expresses misfortune or ruin. بسی yازگار is an adverbial phrase: بسی here functions as 'much/many' (classical usage), یازگار 'again' (repetition), and از شمار 'beyond counting' ('from the count' = beyond number). The verb شد governs the following adverbials to mean 'much was ruined again beyond count.'
segments.s670-g124: نبشته نشد is a compound of a noun/participle (نبشته, 'written record/inscription') with the negative past auxiliary نشد ('was not / did not happen / was not recorded'), so the phrase means 'was not written/recorded'. هم here intensifies with the sense 'even'. بفرجام is a prepositional/temporal phrase 'at/toward the end' and کار is the governed noun 'of the affair' — together: 'not even was anything written about the affair's end.'
segments.s670-g125: This clause contains an existential/negative construction: بدز here (contextually 'the thief' or the act of stealing personified) is the topic, followed by بر نبد راه where نبد ('was not / did not exist') gives the negative existential and راه ('way/path') is the thing absent — literally 'for the thief there was no path'. زان خواسته ('from that desire') is a genitive/relational phrase modifying what path is lacking. Word order places the topic first, then the negation and the absent item, then the source ('from that desire').
segments.s670-g126: گذشته is a perfect/participial notion 'have passed' or 'gone' and combines with the dative بدو ('for him') to show that time elapsed for that person: 'years have passed for him'. سال is the noun 'years', و is the conjunction 'and', and ناکاسته is an adverb/adjective 'undiminished/without abating' describing the state of those years — 'years passed for him and (they were) undiminished.'
segments.s670-g127: ز هنگام is a temporal prepositional phrase 'since the time of ...' or 'from the time of ...'; هنگام governs the proper names ارجاسب و افراسیاب as its objects. In classical Persian the preposition z + هنگام is a standard way to indicate 'from the time of X'.
segments.s670-g128: This is a genitive/partitive phrase: ز دینار و گوهر 'from dinars and jewels' followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('which/that') and the verb خیزد 'rise/spring up'. The final ز آب 'from the water' specifies the source: 'from dinars and gems that spring from the water.' The relative که links the noun phrase to the verb, as in 'that which rises from the water.'
segments.s670-g329: کنون (now) sets the temporal frame. خلعت is the subject 'robe of honour' and آمد is the past verb 'came/has come' (simple past used narratively). سزاوار تو is an adjectival/predicative phrase (literally 'worthy/fitting to you'); Persian often places such modifiers after the verb, so this whole chunk means 'the robe has come, fitting/appropriate for you.'
segments.s670-g330: پسندیده is a past-participle/adjectival use meaning 'pleasing/approved.' و is the simple conjunction 'and.' در خور is a fixed collocation meaning 'worthy of / appropriate to' (در = 'in', خور here forms the phrase 'در خور' = 'befitting'); کار تو 'your work/office' is the noun phrase governed by در خور. Together: 'pleasing and befitting your work.'
segments.s670-g331: چوبنهاد is a compound/verb meaning literally 'set/posted (on a staff)' — here 'posted' or 'had placed.' برنامه is the notice/arrangement/edict being posted. برمهرشاه is a fused prepositional phrase 'on/with the king's seal' (بر + مهرِ شاه) indicating the proclamation bore the royal seal.
segments.s670-g332: بفرمود is the courteous/honorific past 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that') expressing what he ordered. دو کدانی سیاه is the direct object of that order: 'two black k.adānī' (kدانی here = gourd/container); note adjective سیاه follows the noun in the poetic ordering.
segments.s670-g333: بیارند is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'let them bring' / 'they bring' (subjunctive/imperative flavor) used here to introduce what is to be brought. با introduces accompaniment or what is supplied 'with' — دوک و پنبه are 'spindle and cotton.' دروی follows as a genitive/possessive marker: 'the dervish's spindle and cotton' (literal order in poetry places the possessor after the possessed).
segments.s670-g334: نهاده is a perfect/past participle meaning 'having placed' or 'put (on/over)'. بسی is an intensifier 'very/much' (here modifying what follows). ناسزا رنگ وبوی is a compounded phrase: ناسزا qualifies 'رنگ و بوی' (literally 'insulting/abusive colour and scent'), i.e. 'with many offensive colours and odours' — رنگ و بوی is a standard binomial 'colour and smell/appearance and scent.'
segments.s670-g335: هم 'also' adds this list to what was brought. از here means 'of/from' indicating material or source. شعر in this context is an archaic word for fine woven cloth/fabric (not 'poetry' here). پیراهن is the tunic/shirt; لاژورد is the colour/material 'lapis-lazuli (azure),' modifying پیراهن and placed after it in the poetic order.
segments.s670-g336: یکی is the numeral 'one' (= 'a certain' / 'one of them') introducing a single item. سرخ qualifies مقناع: 'a red cowl/veil' (مقناع = hood/face-veil). و connects that item to the next: شلوار زرد 'yellow trousers' (noun + adjective). Adjectives may appear before or after the noun in poetry; here both patterns occur but meaning is straightforward.
segments.s670-g89: سخن‌گر is a noun ('one who speaks' / 'speaker') placed before the verb as topic. نرفتی is a past-form with the negative prefix (you did not go / you did not become); the adjectival/ adverbial phrase بدین گونه سرد ('in this cold manner') follows and specifies how (or in what manner) the speaking would be done. بدین is an archaic compound (ب + این) meaning 'in this way', and سرد here is metaphorical ('cold/aloof') describing tone.
segments.s670-g90: This is an existential/possessive construction with a topical dative: تو را ('for you' / emphatic 'you') + نیستی (there is not / you do not have). دل پرآزار و درد is the nominal predicate (‘a heart full of torment and pain’). Word order is topical dative → negative copula → predicate, so the whole means 'you do not have a heart full of torment and pain.'
segments.s670-g91: بدو ('to him') is an indirect object or dative pronoun marking the addressee; گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing reported speech. کین is the noun 'hatred/vengeance' and بدرگ qualifies it (here taken as 'ill‑disposed/ill‑tempered' or 'base'); بی‌هنر is an adjective 'unskilled/unrefined' modifying the person characterized by that hatred.
segments.s670-g92: بجوید is a present/habitual verb meaning 'seeks/ searches for' (here a general statement about tendency), with همی as an older continuous/emphatic particle ('continually/indeed'). خاک و خون پدر is the compound object 'the father's earth (dust) and blood' — poetic terms for remains or vengeance object. Verb → temporal particle → object order is normal here.
segments.s670-g93: بدو گفت خاقان is 'the khagan said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت = said; خاقان = the khagan). که introduces the subordinate clause (here the content of the speech). این بد مکن is a negative imperative/plea: این ('this') + بد ('bad/evil') + مکن ('do not' — negative imperative of کردن).
segments.s670-g94: This short clause states the consequence: بتیزی is the subject (poetic/archaic noun; sense not supplied here), بزرگی is a nominal attribute 'greatness', and بگردد is a present/future or optative form of گشتن 'will become / may turn into' (poetic form). کهن ('old/ancient') modifies the resulting greatness. The phrase compresses 'this [folly] will become a great old thing.'
segments.s670-g95: بگیتی is an adverbial phrase 'in the world' or 'among people'. هرآنکس که is a relative/headphrase 'whoever' (که introduces the clause). او چون تو بود is the embedded clause: او ('he') + چون تو ('like you') + بود (past copula 'was'). The whole line says 'in the world, anyone who was like you...'.
segments.s670-g96: This is a paired nominal phrase describing condition: سرش ('his head') + پر ز گرد ('full of dust') and ودلش ('and his heart') + پر ز دود ('full of smoke'). The construction 'پر ز X' = 'full of X' is common in poetic Persian; گرد here is 'dust' and دود 'smoke'.
segments.s670-g185: چوخاقان (چو خاقان) here is an adverbial/nominal phrase meaning 'like a khaghan' or functioning as the subject/agent; برفت is the past-tense verb 'برفت' (went/left) though the listed lemma is mismatched — it functions as the main past verb; از در شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the king's gate/door' in which در = 'door/gate' (not 'in'). Word order places the agent phrase before the verb and the directional prepositional phrase after it.
segments.s670-g186: عنانش is 'عنان' (reins) with the enclitic -ش 'his', functioning as the direct object ('his reins'); گرفت is the past verb 'took/seized'; آن زمان is a time expression 'at that time/then' (آن qualifying زمان); پرده‌دار is the noun 'the chamberlain' and here serves as the agent/subject that performs گرفت. The sequence places the object and verb before the explicit subject, a common flexible order in classical Persian.
segments.s670-g187: پیاده شد is a light/periphrastic past expression: پیاده ('on foot') + شد ('became' used as verb) = 'he dismounted' (he became on-foot). از باره is a classical prepositional phrase 'with respect to / concerning the matter/occasion' (باره = 'occasion/instance'); پرموده is a past-participle/adjectival phrase 'well‑taught/experienced' describing the subject; زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the following action. The pieces form a small clause giving manner and qualification before the next verb.
segments.s670-g188: بران is an adverb/discursive particle 'thereupon/thereon/then'; کهتری is an adjective 'lesser/inferior' modifying the following noun; جادوییها (literally 'جادویی' + plural -ها) functions here as a noun phrase referring to 'magical acts/magic(s) or magics' (classical usage is flexible); نمود is the past-tense verb 'showed/did/displayed'. Together the segment means 'thereupon he displayed a lesser magic'; the subject is supplied from context.
segments.s670-g189: پیاده همان شاه is a nominal phrase 'that very king (dis)mounted' with پیاده giving the manner/state and همان emphasizing 'the same/that very'; دستش is 'his hand' (دست + enclitic -ش) and بدست is an idiomatic element meaning 'in (the) hand' or 'hand-to-hand' that here participates in the idiom 'take by the hand'. The phrase sets up a physical action (taking by the hand) which the following verb completes.
segments.s670-g190: بیاورد is the past-tense verb 'brought' (he brought); او را is the definite object 'him' (او + object marker را); بجای نشست is a compound expression 'sat in his place / sat in the seat' (بجای = 'in the place / instead of', نشست = 'sat'). The clause contains two coordinated past actions: 'brought him' and then 'sat in his place.'
segments.s670-g191: خرامان is an adverbial participle/adverb 'with a proud/stately gait'; بیامد is the past-tense verb 'came/approached'; به نزدیک تخت is a directional prepositional phrase 'to the vicinity/near the throne' (به + نزدیک + تخت). The order places manner (خرامان) before the verb and the destination phrase after it.
segments.s670-g192: مراورا is a fused pronoun phrase 'me and him/her' (object grouping); شهنشاه is 'the Shahanshah / emperor' (here part of the coordinated object phrase); بنواخت is the past verb 'struck/struck at' (بنواخت = he smote/struck); سخت is an adverb 'firmly/harshly/strongly' modifying the verb. Word order places the object phrase(s) before the verb, so the clause reads 'he struck (shahanshah and me) hard.'
segments.s670-g321: هنرها (plural) is the subject 'skills/arts/virtues'. ز (poetic for از) is the preposition 'from'. یزدان is the divine source 'God'. نبینی here functions as a present/predictive verb 'you see/you derive' (classical word order places the verb after its complements); همی is the older continuous/emphatic particle that follows the verb to mark habitual/predictive aspect. Together the clause says that the arts originate from God.
segments.s670-g322: به is the directional preposition 'to/onto'. چرخ فلک is a binomial expression for the celestial sphere/sky (both words mean sky/heaven). برنشینی is a compound verb form from بر + نشستن meaning 'to sit up/on' or 'to be raised to a seat' (here with the sense of taking an elevated seat). همی again marks a continuing or emphatic aspect. The whole clause predicts being seated on the heavenly wheel.
segments.s670-g323: ز here is the poetic short form of از 'from'. فرمان (understood inside the fused form) is 'order/command' and من is the possessor 'my'. سربپیچیده‌ای is a perfect/past form with 2nd-person singular ending -‌ای; in classical usage 'سرپیچیدن' (literally 'to turn the head') idiomatically means 'to turn away from, to disobey' — so the clause says 'you have turned away from my command.'
segments.s670-g324: دگرگونه is the adjective 'different/other'. کاری is a noun meaning 'a deed/undertaking' (here 'a course of action'). بسیجیده‌ای is a perfective 2nd-person form from بسیجیدن/bسیج کردن 'to muster/prepare/mobilize' and so means 'you have mustered/prepared' — together the clause says you have prepared a different sort of action.
segments.s670-g325: نیاید is a negative optative/impersonal form meaning 'may ... not come' or 'let ... not come'; همی is the emphatic/continuous particle following the verb. یادت ('your remembrance' or 'that you remember') is the logical subject/affected item. از رنج من is an ablative/agent phrase 'of/from my suffering' (رنج من = 'my toil/suffering'), so the clause prays/commands that the addressee not be reminded by the speaker's hardships.
segments.s670-g326: This is a noun list in apposition to the preceding phrase: سپاه من 'my army/host', و 'and' connects کوشش 'effort/endeavor', وگنج من 'and my treasure/store'. These items are the objects the speaker asks the addressee not to recall; the syntax omits a repeating verb and simply lists the possessed things.
segments.s670-g327: ره پهلوانان is a genitival phrase 'the way/passage of the champions' (ره = 'way/path'). نسازی is the negative present/future 2nd‑person form (ن + سازی) from ساختن/ساختن 'to make' and thus means 'you will not make/you do not make.' همی is the habitual/emphatic particle. The clause says the addressee will not clear or make a way for the heroes.
segments.s670-g328: سرت 'your head' is the subject; به آسمان is the directional phrase 'to the sky/heaven'. برفرازی (here verbal) means 'to raise aloft / to exalt' (bar‑ + فروزی/فرازی with an upward sense) and, with همی, indicates a habitual/predictive 'you will lift/you raise'. The clause states that you will lift your head toward the heavens.
segments.s670-g233: ز is the preposition 'from/because of' placed before گفتار (poetic order). گفتار او is a genitive phrase 'his speech/words' (او = his). شاه شد is a simple past copular construction using شد 'became' and بدگمان is a predicative adjective 'suspicious' describing the king.
segments.s670-g234: روانش is 'his spirit/mind' (noun + possessive -ش). پراندیشه is an adjectival/participial compound 'full of thought, pensive' modifying روانش. بدیک زمان is an adverbial phrase: بدیک 'at once/for a while' + زمان 'time/moment', giving the temporal nuance 'for a short time / for a moment'.
segments.s670-g235: هیونی is the subject (here a beast/creature used as the arriving messenger). بیامد is the past tense 'came'. همانگه is an adverb 'at that instant', placed after the verb for poetic word order. سترگ ('gaze, sight') here is descriptive—poetically indicating a strong look or presence associated with the arrival.
segments.s670-g236: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' introducing the next noun. نامه‌ای is 'a letter' (indefinite with -ای). از marks source 'from', and دبیر بزرگ is a genitive/epithet 'the great scribe/secretary' (دبیر with بزرگ as qualifier).
segments.s670-g237: که here introduces the quoted/formulaic blessing ('that' / 'which' used to lead into the following optative phrase). شاه جهان is a title 'king of the world', جاودان is an adjective 'eternal', and شادباد is an optative fixed formula meaning 'may he be happy' or 'hail'.
segments.s670-g238: همه کار is 'all (his) deeds/affairs' (همه = all, کار = deeds/affairs). اوبخشش is a fused expression meaning 'his forgiveness' or 'that he be forgiven' (او + بخشش). وداد is a noun in the blessing (here part of a paired wish) and باد is the optative particle 'may' — together giving the sense 'may (there be) ...' The exact noun وداد is somewhat poetic/ambiguous but functions as the second element wished upon the king.
segments.s670-g239: چنان دان is an imperative/joinder meaning 'consider/know thus' or 'so think'. که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. برد is a noun 'victory/prize', یمانی is the genitive 'of the Yemeni' (proper or epithet), and دوبود is an archaic/past copula 'was' (classical perfect/past form), completing the subordinate clause 'that the Yemeni's victory was'.
segments.s670-g240: همه موزه is 'the whole hoard/collection' (همه = all; موزه here poetically 'treasure/hoard' though literal senses listed include 'museum'). از marks 'of/from' and گوهر is 'gems/jewels'. نابسود is a negative adjective 'unspoiled / not ruined / not fruitless' describing that treasure. The whole phrase states that all the jewel-hoard remained unstained.
