segments.s670-g161: زلشکر appears here as a fused form meaning 'from the army' (archaic/poetic z + لشکر); it precedes the verb as a prepositional/partitive phrase. گزین کرد is a light-verb construction: گزین (choose/select) is the lexical verb and کرد is the past tense auxiliary 'made/did', so 'chose'. مردی is the object 'a thousand men' (mردی = 'man' but here part of the numeral phrase 'مردی هزار' = 'a thousand men'). Word order is topical NP + verb + object.
segments.s670-g162: که introduces a relative/purpose clause 'who/that'. با is the simple preposition 'with'. اوشود is an older/poetic fused form (او شود) here functioning as present/jussive-like 'be with him' or 'be to be with him' (literal list gives 'he/she became' but context gives a stative 'be'). تا marks the purpose/result clause 'so that/in order that'. درشهریار is a locative compound 'in the king's presence' (در + شهریار). The whole clause explains the role/purpose of the chosen men.
segments.s670-g163: زخاقان (خاقان with an epenthetic z) is the subject 'the khagan/ruler'. شتر (camel) is the direct object of خواست, and خواست is the past-tense verb 'asked/wanted/requested'. کاروان follows as a goal/scene word 'for the caravan' — syntactically the sequence names what was requested and the context (a caravan). Word order is Subject + Object + Verb (+ complement).
segments.s670-g164: شمرد is the past tense 'he counted' (verb). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'then/at that time' (آن here functions adverbially with زمان). جمله means 'all' or 'in all' and is the quantified complement to شمرد. بر ساروان is a prepositional phrase 'upon/to the caravan‑leaders' (بر + ساروان) indicating the distribution or assignment. The clause states the counting/allocation done at that time.
segments.s670-g165: سواران is the subject 'the riders/horsemen'. پس پشت is an adverbial phrase 'at the rear/behind' (پس = then/after; پشت = rear). وخاقان is 'and the khagan' (و + خاقان) linking the next topical element. زپیش (ز + پیش) means 'from the front' or 'at the front' in this poetic spelling. The sequence arranges spatial positions (rear vs. front) by placing the noun then the locative/positional phrase.
segments.s670-g166: همی‌راند is an imperfective past/perfective-continuous form: the progressive prefix همی- plus the past-stem راند (to drive/ride) yields 'was riding/driving on'. با is the preposition 'with'. نامداران is the plural adjective/noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones' (here 'famed companions'). خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'his own' split into خوی + ش (possessive clitic); together they mean 'his companions'. The clause describes accompaniment and possession.
segments.s670-g167: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. خاقان is the subject 'the khagan'. بیامد is the past tense 'came'. به نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'to/near the king' where به + نزدیک gives the directional/locative sense and شاه is the object of that location. This is a standard time + subject + verb + locative sequence.
segments.s670-g168: ابا is an archaic/poetic term here; in context it behaves like a verb or verbal preposition meaning 'bringing/bearing' (the lexical senses provided in the list do not match neatly, so this is an idiomatic/archaising usage). گنج دیرینه is the noun phrase 'the ancient/long‑standing treasure' (گنج = treasure; دیرینه = ancient). و با سپاه is a coordination 'and with an army' (و = and; با سپاه = with an army). The whole segment lists what the khagan brought with him.
segments.s670-g193: بپرسید is a past tense / perfect-like verb meaning 'he asked' (3rd‑person). The conjunction و simply links the next verb. بنشاختش is an older/poetic causative/perfect form meaning 'he set him to sit' or 'he seated him'; the clitic -ش = 'him' is attached to that verb. پیش خویش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of/near his own self' (پیش = 'in the presence of'; خویش = 'his own / himself'), specifying where he seated the person.
segments.s670-g194: بگفتند is a simple past 'they said'. بسیار here functions as an adverb 'greatly / far / very much'. ز انداره بیش is an idiomatic poetic expression meaning 'beyond measure' (ز = 'from/of', انداره = 'measure/limit', بیش = 'excess / too much'), so the phrase reports that they declared him to exceed all proper measure.
segments.s670-g195: سزاوار functions as an adjective 'suitable/appropriate'. When followed by او it forms 'suitable for him' (سزاوار او). جایگه is the object 'place/position' and ساختند is the past plural verb 'they made/constructed', so the clause literally reads 'they made a fitting place for him.' Note the adjective precedes the beneficiary (او) in this word order.
segments.s670-g196: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain', here introducing a single item. خرم modifies ایوان as an attributive adjective 'joyful / pleasant / splendid iwan'. بپرداختند (پرداختند with an initial ب-) in this context means 'they attended to / wrought / adorned / set up' rather than literal 'they paid', so the whole clause means 'they set up/finished a pleasant iwan.'
segments.s670-g197: ببردند is a past tense verb 'they carried/brought/removed' (here 'they brought'). چیزی که شایسته بود is a relative clause 'something which was fitting/appropriate' (که introduces the relative clause; شایسته 'worthy/appropriate'; بود = past 'was'). Grammatically, چیزی (object) is modified by the following relative clause.
segments.s670-g198: همان is a demonstrative/adjective 'that same / the very (thing)'. پیش followed by a noun (پرموده) gives 'in the presence of / before the experienced one' (پرموده = 'experienced / well‑versed'). بایسته بود is a predicative phrase 'was fitting / was proper' (بایسته = 'proper/necessary'; بود = past 'was'), so the clause states that that very item was proper to present before the experienced person.
segments.s670-g199: سپه را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the army' as direct object). به نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase 'near him' (به + نزدیک + او). جای کرد is a verbal compound 'placed / set in position' (جای = place, کرد = did/made), so the clause reads 'they placed the army near him.'
segments.s670-g200: دبیری is a noun 'the office/secretariat (of secretary)'. The following phrase is idiomatic/older structure: بدان (here tokenized as ب + دان) + کاربر means 'to/for that worker or for that task', and پای کرد (پای = 'base/foot', کرد = 'made/did') is a fixed verbal phrase meaning 'established/fixed'. Together it means 'he established/appointed the secretaryship to that worker / for that task.' This is an older, somewhat compact way to say 'appointed a scribe/secretary for the work.'
segments.s670-g201: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آگه شد is a verbal phrase: آگه (aware) + شد (past of شدن 'to become'), so 'became aware'. از marks the topic/source 'of/regarding', followed by the noun phrase کار آن خواسته 'that affair/request' (کار = 'affair/matter', آن = 'that', خواسته = 'what was asked for'). Word order is typical Persian: preposition (از) before its noun.
segments.s670-g202: که is the relative/that-clause introducer here ('that/which'). آورد is the simple past 3rd‑person of آوردن 'to bring' ('he/they brought'). پرموده آراسته are two postpositive adjectives (both follow what they modify in this poetic line) describing the thing/person brought: پرموده 'seasoned/experienced' and آراسته 'neatly arranged/ornamented'.
segments.s670-g203: به میدان فرستاده is a phrase with به + میدان 'to the field' and فرستاده which functions as 'sent' (past participle / verb form). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that'), and همچنان 'likewise/in the same manner' links this purpose to the prior action.
segments.s670-g204: برد here is an archaic/poetic verbal form related to بردن 'to carry/take' used in a purpose clause after تا ('so that they would carry'); بار پرمایه is a noun + adjective ('a load full of substance/valuable cargo') with the adjective after the noun as usual, and با ساروان is a prepositional phrase 'with the caravan‑drivers' indicating who accompanies/carries it.
segments.s670-g205: چوآسود is a fused poetic form meaning 'when (he) rested' (چو + آسود), and پرموده modifies the person as 'seasoned/experienced'. از marks the source/cause 'from', so از رنج راه means 'from the hardship/suffering of the road'.
segments.s670-g206: بهشتم here functions as به + (a court/royal place) 'to/at the court' (به = 'to/for'). یکی = 'one/a', سور = 'feast/banquet', and فرمود is the verb meaning 'ordered/commanded' (here used with شاه as the subject). Note the poetic word order with the verb before its subject (فرمود شاه = 'the king commanded').
segments.s670-g207: چو 'when' introduces the clause. The sequence خاقان زپیش جهاندار شاه lists high titles/titles in apposition: خاقان (khagan), زپیش (an adverbial/form meaning 'from before' or a poetic connective), جهاندار 'world‑holder' and شاه 'king' — a compact string of epithets typical in epic poetry.
segments.s670-g208: نشستند is the past 3rd‑person plural of نشستن 'they sat/took their seats'. برخوان means 'at the table/at the banquet', او = 'his/him' (referring back to the host), and پیش گاه is a collocation meaning 'in the presence of the court/at the royal presence' (پیش before + گاه 'place/time'). Note the plural verb matches the coordinated/high officials named earlier.
segments.s670-g97: همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone'. بد سگالید functions as a predicate: سگالید is an older poetic/archaic verb form meaning 'be hostile/ill‑disposed' (3rd‑person plural) with بد as qualifying adjective. وباکس نساخت splits into با کس ('with anyone/with anyone at all') before the verb, and نساخت is the past tense of ساخت with the negative prefix ن‑, so 'did not make/form (a pact/relationship)'. Word order (object phrase + verb) is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s670-g98: بکژی is an archaic/poetic verbal/adjectival item meaning something like 'go perverse / be wrong' (here rendered as 'perversity/arrogance arose'); it links by و to نابخردی ('and folly'). سر is the noun 'head' and فراخت is a past verb ('lifted/rose'), so the two preceding nouns behave as subjects that 'lifted their heads'—a common idiom for 'arose' or 'became prominent.'
segments.s670-g99: همی is an adverbial particle meaning 'always/continually' placed before the prepositional phrase ازشهنشاه ('of/from the Shahanshah'), which marks the cause/agent of the fear. ترسانییم is an archaic first‑person plural verbal/adjectival form ('we are frightened / we are afraid') with the old ‑ییم ending; the construction means 'we are continually afraid of the king.'
segments.s670-g100: سزا is a noun 'desert/just punishment'; ز (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'from/of' and pairs with زو = 'from him'. بود is the past copula 'was', so سزا زو بود = 'the due (from him) was ...'. رنج is the noun 'suffering' as the complement of بود. و introduces the next clause; آسانییم is another archaic predicate form (we‑form with ‑ییم) meaning 'we are at ease' or 'there was ease for us', so the clause contrasts 'his due was suffering, and for us (there was) ease.'
segments.s670-g101: زگردنکشان is a poetic genitive/prepositional phrase ('toward/from the neck‑cutters' = 'toward those who cut throats / the slayers'). اوهمال is a fused adjective meaning 'he is negligent/indulgent' (او + هِمال) describing the subject's attitude toward them. منست is an older copular/predicative form ('is / it is fitting'), so the whole phrase states he is indulgent toward those murderers.
segments.s670-g102: نه چون is the negative comparative 'not like'; بنده is the noun 'servant'. اوبدسگال is a compound adjective (او بدسگال) meaning 'his servant is ill‑disposed/ill‑fated/hostile'. منست again is the archaic copula/predicate 'is', so the clause says 'not like his servant, (who) is ill‑disposed.'
segments.s670-g103: This is a stacked adjectival phrase: هشیوار ('alert/attentive') followed by و آهسته ('and gentle/slow‑moving/soft') and و با نژاد ('and of lineage'). با + نژاد literally 'with lineage' = 'of (good) birth/lineage'. The coordinating و's simply link the qualities describing the same person.
segments.s670-g104: بسی here is an intensifier 'much/very/many' (poetic usage). نامبردار is 'renowned/famed' (adjective or noun 'famous names'), دارد is the present auxiliary/verb 'has/keeps', and بیاد literally 'in memory' or 'in mind' (so دارد بیاد = 'keeps in mind' or 'remembers'). Together: 'He keeps many renowned names in memory.'
segments.s670-g225: This clause places the speech: بآیین گشسب is an adpositional/phrase identifying the addressee (here the name/epithet Ayin Gushasp) or manner, آن زمان gives the time ('at that time'), and شاه گفت is the core 'the king said'. Word order is time/adressee + subject + verb; گفت is used as the past tense verb 'said'.
segments.s670-g226: که introduces a content clause ('that...'). با او = 'with/ toward him' (the preposition + pronoun), بد + ش is 'his bad/ill (feeling)' where ش is the possessive suffix, آشکار is an adjective 'apparent/open', و ties the two predicates, نهفت is 'hidden/concealed'. The clause says his ill-feeling was both manifest and concealed.
segments.s670-g227: که begins a subordinate clause ('that'/'for'), چون + بینی is a temporal/conditional 'when you see' (چون = 'when/as', بینی = 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you see'), این کار چوبینه را is the direct object phrase 'this wooden (cudgel/thing)' with را marking definiteness/object; چوبینه is postpositive adjective 'wooden/club-like.'
segments.s670-g228: بمردی is an adverbial phrase 'manfully, bravely'. به کار آورد is the verb phrase 'bring into use/put into practice' (به + کار + آورد). کینه را is the definite object 'the grudge' (را marks the object). The overall instruction: employ the grudge with manliness.
segments.s670-g229: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (a formulaic reporting phrase). آیین گشسب دبیر is a name + title: 'Ayin Gushasp, the scribe' (proper name followed by office). Word order: reporting phrase followed by the speaker's name/title.
segments.s670-g230: که‌ای here is a vocative/exclamatory address ('O' + vocative particle) followed by شاه 'king' (vocative). روشن‌دل is an adjective 'bright-hearted/clear-minded' and یادگیر is an epithet 'learner/quick to learn' — two attributes joined by و. This is direct address to the king.
segments.s670-g231: بسوری ('to/at the feast') sets the scene. که introduces a relative/claused remark 'where/whose', دستانش = 'his hands' (دستان + possessive -ش), چوبین is an adjectival predicate 'club-like/wooden', and بود is past 'was'. The clause describes someone at the feast as having club-like hands.
segments.s670-g232: چنان دان is an imperative/formula 'consider it so' or 'know thus' (دان from دانستن used as an instruction). که introduces the content: خوانش نو = 'a new recitation/reading', آیین بود = 'was a ritual/custom' (بود = past 'was'). The clause tells the listener to regard the recitation as a new rite.
segments.s670-g401: بدو گفت = “he said to him.” بدو is an older/poetic form of به او (to him) placed before the verb, and گفت here is the past-tense verb “said” (the provided lemma senses are nouny, so the verbal use is not among those senses). کین خود مگوی is a prohibition: مگوی is the negative imperative/subjunctive “do not say/tell,” کین is the object “hatred/vengeance,” and خود is emphatic “own” (i.e. “your own hatred”).
segments.s670-g402: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause (“for/because”). از شاه marks source/agent “from the king,” گیرد is a present/future-oriented third-person verb “takes/seizes” (classical Persian uses this form to state a consequence), and سپاه آبروی is a genitive-like phrase “the army’s honor/reputation” (word order here is poetic; the noun phrase functions as the object of گیرد).
segments.s670-g403: همه سر به سر is an idiom meaning “one and all” / “all together.” بندگان وییم = “we are his servants” (وییم = we are of him / we belong to him); the clause uses classical word order with the predicate following the noun phrase and an enclitic-style verbal form وییم for “we are.”
segments.s670-g404: دهنده‌ست is a nominal predicate meaning “he is the giver” (subject is omitted but understood from context). و connects the clauses; خواهان/خواهندگان means “those who desire/seek,” and وییم again is “we are” (so the second clause reads “and we are the seekers”). Note the predicate دهنده‌ست describes the ruler as generous, while خواهندگان وییم states the speakers’ relation.
segments.s670-g405: چنین یافت پاسخ ز ایرانیان = “thus (چنین) they found/received (یافت) an answer (پاسخ) from (ز, poetic for از) the Iranians (ایرانیان).” یافت is a past-tense verb “found/received” and ز is the archaic poetic form of از meaning “from.”
segments.s670-g406: که introduces reported content (“that …”). ماخود is emphatic “we ourselves.” نبندیم is first-person-plural negative/subjunctive “we will not bind/tie” (classical form used to express future or resolved action). زین‌پس is a compound adverb “henceforth / from now on” (زین = saddle; پس = after → idiomatically “from this saddle forward”), and میان “between” completes the verb’s idea (the sentence is elliptical about what exactly is between, but grammatically میان modifies نبندیم).
segments.s670-g407: به ایران sets the location/topic “in (or as regards) Iran.” کس اورا نخوانیم شاه is a negative declarative: نخوانیم is first-person-plural “we will not call/sing/read,” اورا is the object pronoun “him,” کس here functions idiomatically with negation to mean “(no one) …” or “we will not call him anyone (king),” and شاه is the predicate noun “king” (word order is poetic/inverted compared with modern prose).
segments.s670-g408: نه = “nor.” بهرام را پهلوان سپاه is an echo/parallel clause where the verb is omitted (understood from the previous clause): بهرام را (Bahrām with definite object marker را) پهلوان سپاه = “Bahram (as) the champion/hero of the army.” The sense is “nor will we call Bahram the army’s champion.”
segments.s670-g281: This is a nominal clause with the patient/thing first: یکی خلعت آراسته بود شاه = 'one robe (adorned) was (the) king'. آراسته (past participle/adjective) + بود is a periphrastic past (بود here functions as an auxiliary), and شاه at the end is the subject (classical Persian often places the subject after the verbal phrase). یکی is an indefinite 'a/certain one'.
segments.s670-g282: ز introduces material/quality here (poetic 'of/with'); زرین و سیمین are coordinating adjectives 'golden and silvery' describing the robe. The following و‑phrase lists further items (اسب وکلاه = 'a horse and a cap'); note older orthography sometimes fuses و with the following word (وسیمین, وکلاه).
segments.s670-g283: Standard word order: prepositional phrase 'به نزدیک خاقان' ('to/near the khagan') precedes the verb فرستاد ('sent'); شاه at the end is the subject. به here marks direction/recipient.
segments.s670-g284: دومنزل is a compound numeral/classifier meaning 'two stages' or 'for two day/stage's travel'; همی‌رفت is the imperfective/past-continuous classical form ('was going' / 'proceeded'), and با او به راه is a prepositional phrase 'with him on the road'. The clause therefore describes movement accompanying someone for two stages.
segments.s670-g285: سه دیگر = 'the other three' (three others). نپیمود is past negative (ن‑ + پیمود) = 'did not traverse/undertake'. راه دراز = 'the long road' (noun + adjective; adjective follows noun in this classical order).
segments.s670-g286: درودش is the object 'his greeting' with the enclitic -ش ('to him/his'), followed by the verb فرستاد 'sent'. وزو is an older contracted sequence (و زو = و از او) meaning 'and from him / and then away from him' (here the provided senses list doesn't match this classical contraction), and گشت باز = 'turned back / returned' (گشت = 'turned/went', باز = 'back/again').
segments.s670-g287: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (classical usage); آگاهی آمد = 'news/information came' (آمد is simple past). سوی پهلوان = 'to/toward the champion' (سوی marks motion toward someone). Word order places the time/conjunction first, then the verbal clause.
segments.s670-g288: ازان is a demonstrative/partitive 'of that / from that' linking to the noun phrase خلعت شهریار جهان = 'the robe of the sovereign (شهریار) of the world (جهان)'. This is a genitive/possessive chain describing which robe the news concerned.
segments.s670-g17: بگفتند is a past tense reporting verb ('they said'). The clause that follows is an optative wish built around the noun phrase 'کین رنج' (literally 'this/that rancor / pain') and the past form دادی ('you gave' / 'you inflicted'), with the final word بباد functioning as an optative/jussive passive-ish verb meaning 'may it be blown away' (an archaic poetic wish form). Word order: reporting verb + reported clause; the modal/wish verb comes sentence-final as typical in older Persian poetry.
segments.s670-g18: This is a short wish-sentence: 'سر نامور' = 'the famed/renowned head' (noun + postpositive adjective), 'پر ز آتش' = 'full of fire' where پر (full) takes the poetry-form preposition ز ('from/of'), and the final مباد is the negative optative ('may it not be'). So the whole line literally: 'May the famed head not be full of fire.'
segments.s670-g19: بدانست is a past-tense verb meaning 'he knew' or 'he perceived.' The following proper name بهرام is the subject (classical Persian can place the verb before the subject). کان here is a classical particle introducing the content of perception ('that' / 'as if'), followed by بود زشت = 'was ugly' (بود = past copula, زشت = predicate adjective).
segments.s670-g20: باب is the noun (here best read as 'gate/door' or similar object) and اندرافگند is a past transitive verb meaning 'cast into / put in.' The prefixed وَ (surface و‌تر here treated as the conjunction 'and' / 'then') connects clauses; گشت is the past of 'become/turn' and خشت ('brick') is the noun of the final clause. The clause sequence is narrative past: he cast X into Y, and (then) the brick was turned/changed.
segments.s670-g21: پشیمان شد is a simple past copular construction 'he became regretful' (پشیمان = regretful + شد = became). The conjunction و links the next action; او is the object pronoun 'him / his' placed before برگرفت (classical order), and برگرفت is past 'took up / seized.' So: 'he grew repentant and took up/took back his bond/pledge.'
segments.s670-g22: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' and introduces the reason ('because of his deed' = کردار خود). دست بر سر گرفت is an idiomatic verb phrase: دست (hand) + بر (on/onto) + سر گرفت (took to the head) = 'he put his hand on his head' (an expression of grief or remorse). سرگرفت here is a compound verb; the whole phrase is a past action consequence of remorse.
segments.s670-g23: فرستاد is the past tense 'he sent/dispatched.' The object follows: اسبی (a horse) modified by بزرین (great) and ستام (a participial/adjectival epithet meaning 'praised/famed'). Classical Persian frequently places adjectives after the noun. This is a compact noun phrase 'a great, praised horse' as the thing sent.
segments.s670-g24: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' introducing the next gift; تیغ هندی is 'an Indian sword' (noun + adjective), followed by بزرین نیام where بزرین modifies نیام ('a large scabbard'). The sequence is a terse list of items sent: 'a certain Indian sword, (with) a great scabbard.'
segments.s670-g153: This is a noun phrase: دو (two) + برد یمانی (a compound nominal: برد + the adjective/epithet یمانی) followed by the modifier همه زربفت (all/both entirely gold-brocaded). In classical Persian the adjective or adjectival phrase follows the noun it qualifies; زربفت here describes the material/ornamentation.
segments.s670-g154: بسختند is a past/pluperfect-type plural predicate describing the state of the items (an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning ‘were hard/firmly made’). هر یک بمن بود هفت is a small clause: هر یک (‘each one’) + بمن (‘to me’) + بود (past ‘was’), here meaning ‘each was (valued) seven (for me)’; Persian places the possessor/indirect object (بمن) before the copula بود.
segments.s670-g155: A coordination naming two persons: سپهبد (title 'spahbed') followed by two names joined with و. In classical narrative titles often precede personal names. Here زکشی is given as a lexical item that functions as a name in context.
segments.s670-g156: نبود (negated past of بودن) introduces the predicate ‘were not’. آگه از جستن داوری is a nominal phrase functioning as the complement: آگه ‘aware’ + از (here ‘of/concerning’) + جستن داوری ‘seeking judgment’ (جستن is the infinitive ‘to seek’ and داوری is the noun ‘judgment’). Word order places the negated copula before the adjective phrase.
segments.s670-g157: Another nominal phrase: دو برد یمانی as before, followed by بیکسونهاد — an archaic/periphrastic past participial/causative form meaning ‘left without anyone’ / ‘left solitary/neglected’. The verb form is past and applies to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s670-g158: This clause says what he did not do: دو موزه (two mūza/items) + به نامه (into/to the book/record; به + noun), نکرد (did not do/make) + ایچ یاد (‘any/any at all of a memorial/mention’). Note classical negation: نکرد (past negative) governs the direct object phrase that follows (ایچ یاد = no mention).
segments.s670-g159: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning ‘he said/commanded’. زان پس که introduces a subordinate clause with temporal sense: زان پس (‘after that’) + که (that/when). پیداگشسب is the embedded predicate (an archaic/compounded verb form meaning ‘was found / became visible’). The whole segment is the reporting verb + temporal clause introducing what was ordered.
segments.s670-g160: همی here is a classical marker of ongoing/habitual/intentional aspect often used before verbs in Ferdowsi; با سواران is a prepositional phrase ‘with the horsemen’, and نشیند is a jussive/subjunctive-present form meaning ‘let him sit/ride’ or ‘he should sit/ride’ (used after a command). براسب is an adverbial ‘on horseback’ and follows the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s670-g353: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that'/'that'); خلعت is the direct object 'a robe of honour' placed before the verb in classical word order; ازینسان modifies خلعت ('in this manner' / 'such as this'); فرستد is 3rd-person singular in a classical/purpose sense (subjunctive/optative reading: 'may send' / 'would send'); بمن is the indirect object 'to me' following the verb.
segments.s670-g354: بدان is an archaic/contracted locution (literally 'به آن' = 'by that' / 'thereby'); تا here marks purpose ('so that' / 'in order that'); ببینند is 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative 'they may see' (used with تا for intended/resultive action); هر انجمن is 'every/each assembly' as the group meant to observe.
segments.s670-g355: جهاندار is the subject 'the world-ruler/sovereign'; بر بندگان is a prepositional phrase 'over / against the servants' indicating the domain of rule; پادشاست is the nominal predicate 'is king' (پادشاه + است contracted), stating his sovereignty.
segments.s670-g356: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; مر مرا is a classical doubled pronominal sequence where مر functions as an archaic dative 'to me' and مرا is the accusative/emphatic 'me' (together emphasizing the person affected); خوار گیرد = 'take / make (someone) despised' — گیرد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'may seize / may take' used for potential action here; رواست is 'it is permissible/fitting' (روا + -ست copula), the evaluative conclusion of the condition.
segments.s670-g357: گمانی is 'a thought/guess' used as the object in an idiom with the negated verb to mean 'I did not imagine/think'; نبردم is the negated past 1st-person 'I did not take/have' but idiomatically 'I did not suppose'; که introduces the subordinate clause; نزدیک شاه is an adverbial phrase 'near the king' (نزدیک modifying spatial relation).
segments.s670-g358: بداندیشگان is a plural agent noun 'those of bad intent' / 'malefactors'; تیز here has adverbial force 'swiftly/quickly' (classical texts commonly use adjectives adverbially); یابند is 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative 'they may find' / 'they will find' (used to express expected or potential outcome); راه is the object 'a way / a path' — subject (بداندیشگان) + manner (تیز) + verb (یابند) + object (راه) is a typical classical ordering.
segments.s670-g359: ولیکن is the contrastive conjunction 'but/yet'; چوهرمز is the proper name of the agent (Hormoz) and functions as the subject; مرا is the accusative pronoun 'me'; خوار is the predicative adjective 'despised' (object complement); کرد is the perfective past 'made/did' — together 'X مرا خوار کرد' = 'X made me despised / humiliated me.'
segments.s670-g360: به is a preposition marking manner/instrument ('by / through / according to'); گفتار آهرمنان is a genitive phrase 'the speech/words of the Ahrimans' (آهرمنان = demons/Ahrimanic advisers); کارکرد here is a classical compound verb form (کار کردن) in the perfective/past sense 'he acted / he behaved' or 'he did so' — literally 'he acted by the words of the devils.'
segments.s670-g113: This is a compact verbal clause with the past tense verb before its object, typical of Ferdowsi's poetic word-order: نبشتند (they wrote) followed by نامه (a letter) and the indirect object marked by the preposition بـ fused to شاه (بشاه = to the king). جهان is an epithet (ʻking of the worldʼ) following شاه. Together: 'they wrote a letter to the king of the world.'
segments.s670-g114: This noun phrase gives the content of the letter: سخن 'word/content' qualified by هرچ بد 'whatever bad (things)' and then the paired adjectives آشکار ونهان 'manifest and hidden'. هرچ is a universal/indefinite modifier ('whatever/every'), آشکار and نهان form a contrasting pair (open vs. hidden). Note the poetic fusion 'ونهان' = و + نهان.
segments.s670-g115: A noun phrase in apposition: سپهدار (the spahbed/commander) followed by the preposition با (with) and موبد موبدان. In poetry the ezāfe or connecting vowel may be dropped, so موبد موبدان functions as 'the mōbed of mōbeds' or 'the chief priest(s)' accompanying the spahbed.
segments.s670-g116: This is an adverbial/time-comment plus reported-speech marker and a vocative: بخشم here is best read as an adverbial phrase indicating 'in anger / angrily' (poetic compression), آن زمان = 'at that time/then', گفت = 'said' introduces the speech, and کای بخردان is the vocative 'O wise ones'. Note some lexical items are compressed or archaic in Ferdowsi and punctuation is supplied by context.
segments.s670-g117: An urgent command-like clause: هم‌اکنون 'right now' sets time, از ایدر 'from here' is the source phrase, followed by بدز (imperative or narrative infinitive of دزدیدن 'to steal off/leave covertly') and درشوید (the plural imperative/command form of شدن with the prefix در- 'go in/through' or 'set off'). Together they form a short command: 'At once, slip away from here.'
segments.s670-g118: This is a two-part imperative: بکوشید is the polite/plural imperative 'strive/try', و links the clauses, and با باد همبر شوید literally 'become companions with the wind' (با + noun + شوید, plural imperative of شدن). The phrase is idiomatic: 'make haste and go with the wind' — i.e. flee swiftly.
segments.s670-g119: A purposive verb phrase: بدز here is used as the action 'they stole off/ slipped away', بر is a directional/preverbal particle often used with motion verbs (≈ 'off to/away to'), and ببیند is the subjunctive/purpose form 'to see'. تا introduces the purpose/result clause that follows: 'so that (they) might see/learn.'
segments.s670-g120: This is the content clause explaining the purpose: چه مایه 'what sort/amount' + بود (past copula 'was') + گنج آراسته 'the arranged/adorned treasure'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: literally 'what amount was (the) treasure prepared.'
segments.s670-g105: به here introduces an oath or prepositional phrase ('by'); جان و سرشاه ایران is a coordinated noun phrase 'the life and sovereign/head of Iran' (جان = 'life', سرشاه = 'sovereign, head of the king'). سپاه (army) follows and functions as the substantive/topic that the following relative clause will modify.
segments.s670-g106: کز introduces a relative clause ('which/that'); ایدر کنون gives the local‑temporal setting ('from here now'); بازگردی is the verbal element ('returns / will return') describing سپاه; به راه is the directional phrase 'to the road/way'. Word order places the relative introducer first, then place/time, then the verb.
segments.s670-g107: بپاسخ functions adverbially ('in reply' / 'as an answer'); نیفزایی is a negative injunction ('do not increase / do not add'), an imperative/negative present telling the listener not to escalate; وبدخوی ('and ill‑natured') is adjectival but here joined with the negative sense to mean 'nor be ill‑tempered' (the negative is understood to extend to it).
segments.s670-g108: نگویی is a negative imperative/formal prohibition ('do not say' / 'do not speak'); سخن is the object 'word(s) / speech'; نیز here means 'also / even'; تا introduces a temporal limit 'until'; نشنوی is the complementary verb 'you hear' (do not speak until you hear). The clause therefore forbids speaking before listening.
segments.s670-g109: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; چوبشنید is fused form meaning 'when he heard' (past tense); بهرام is the proper name and logical subject; زوگشت is a compound meaning 'departed/turned away from that (or him)' (زو = 'from it/him' + گشت = 'turned/departed'); باز is an adverb 'again' modifying the action.
segments.s670-g110: بلشکر is ba+sepah 'with the army' (indicating accompaniment or location); گه آمد literally 'came to the place' (گه here = place/time, آمد = 'came'); گو introduces an epithet/appositive (literally 'called' / 'said'), and رزمساز is an epithet 'war‑maker' or 'war‑instigator' applied to the person who came. The phrase therefore says he came to the army camp, named/described as the war‑maker.
segments.s670-g111: چو again is 'when'; خراد is used adjectivally 'wise' modifying the following name برزین (a personal name), so the phrase reads 'the wise Barzin'; وآن بخردان means 'and those wise men' (آن = 'those', بخردان = 'the wise/prudent ones'), forming a coordinated subject group.
segments.s670-g112: دبیر بزرگ is an appositive 'the great scribe / senior secretary' (دبیر = scribe/secretary, بزرگ = great/elder). ودگر موبدان means 'and the other mobeds' (ودگر linking 'and/other', موبدان = plural of mobed, Zoroastrian priests). The whole segment gives titles/appellations for the previously named wise men.
segments.s670-g81: بجوشید is a past/poetic third-person form of جوشیدن ('to boil') here used figuratively — 'boiled/seethed' with emotion. بهرام is the subject (the proper name); وشد is the conjunction + copula 'and became' (و + شد). زردروی is a predicate adjective 'sallow-faced' describing the result after شد.
segments.s670-g82: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد ('did' → 'looked'). خراد here functions as an epithet 'wise' modifying the following proper name برزین (the wise Barzin). بروی is a short/poetic locative-directional piece (به روی / 'toward the face' / 'at him') completing the verb phrase.
segments.s670-g83: بترسید here is an exhortation/imperative-like form 'fear' (or 'be afraid'), addressed broadly (2nd plural/polite) or used narratively. زان = از آن ('of that' / 'that'), and تیزخونخوار مرد is a compound epithet: تیزخونخوار 'blood‑devouring' qualifying مرد 'man'.
segments.s670-g84: که is the relative 'who/that' introducing a clause. اورا is the object pronoun 'him'. زباد + اندرآرد is a verb phrase where اندرآرد means 'causes' or 'throws into', and زباد in older poetry functions adverbially/intensely (here giving an instrument/forceful sense). بگرد is the verb 'to turn/whirl' (here describing being turned/whirled away). The whole clause says that this man would cause him to be turned/whirled off.
segments.s670-g85: ببهرام گفت is a short phrase 'he said to Bahram' (ببهرام = 'to/for Bahram'; گفت = 'said' in narrative). ای is the vocative 'O' and سزاوار گاه is a vocative phrase meaning roughly 'worthy/appropriate (for the) time' — here addressing Bahram as 'O fitting one / at the fitting time'.
segments.s670-g86: بخور literally 'eat' (imperative) is used idiomatically with خشم 'anger' — 'harbor' or 'nurse anger' (i.e., be angry/take in your wrath). وسر ('and the head') with بازگردان ('turn back/return') and ز راه ('from the road') yields a command 'turn your head away from the road' (بازگردان = causative/imperative 'cause to turn back' or 'turn back'). Together the clause is an imperative counsel: take in your anger and avert your head from the path.
segments.s670-g87: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. خاقان is the subject 'the Khagan' (ruler). همی is an archaic continuous/present habitual marker meaning 'always' or 'indeed', راست گوید is 'speaks true' (راست 'true' + گوید 'says'), and سخن is the noun 'words' — 'that the Khagan always speaks true words.'
segments.s670-g88: توبنیوش is an imperative/optative 'you (should) drink' (بنوش = 'drink') used metaphorically here: 'take (these words) in / accept them'. وانديشه 'and thought' + بدمکن is an injunction 'do not do evil / do not entertain' — so the clause instructs: accept the words and do not form bad thoughts.
segments.s670-g65: This clause functions as a nominalized proposition: "سخن‌گر نیفزایی" = "that the speaker not add (more)". "نیفزایی" is an older negative subjunctive/imperative form meaning "do not add" (addressed in the subordinate clause), and "اکنون رواست" is the main predicate: "it is fitting now" (رواست = "it is proper/fit"). Word order places the subordinate phrase before the copula, a common classical Persian pattern.
segments.s670-g66: The conjunction "که" introduces a causal/explanatory clause: "because/for that...". "آن بد که شد" is a relative clause "that misfortune which occurred" (the second "که" is the relative "which"); "شد" and "گشت" are perfective verbs used together for emphasis ("happened/was and turned/was"), and "با باد راست" (= "with the wind straight/directly") describes how it was carried away or changed by the wind.
segments.s670-g67: "زخاقان چوبشنید" is a temporal clause: "زخاقان" (the khaghan) + the fused form "چو+شنید" (چوبشنید) = "when he heard"; "بهرام گفت" is the main clause "Bahram said". The temporal subordinate comes before the reporting clause, a normal word order in classical Persian.
segments.s670-g68: The conjunction "که" introduces reported speech/complement: "that I thought...". "پنداشتم" is first-person past "I thought/imagined"; "کین بماند نهفت" = "that this hatred/grudge would remain hidden" — "کین" (grudge/vengeance) is the thing thought about, "بماند" is a subjunctive/optative sense of "remain", and "نهفت" is adjectival/participial "hidden/concealed" modifying کین.
segments.s670-g69: "کنون زان گنه‌گر بیاید زیان" is a potential/conditional clause: "کنون" (now) + "زان گنه‌گر" (from that sinner) + "بیاید" (present-subjunctive/potential "may/should come") + "زیان" (harm). The verb بیاید marks the possibility rather than a simple past.
segments.s670-g70: "نپوشم" is a negated first-person form (here: "I will not cover/conceal"). The following imperative "برو" (go) takes the object "چادر پرنیان" (a silken cloak/veil); the verb "bring" or similar is implied and omitted (ellipsis), so the sequence reads as a command to fetch the silken cloak after stating one will not conceal the harm.
segments.s670-g71: "چوآنجارسی" = "when you reach there" (temporal clause). "هرچ باید بگوی" = "say whatever must be said" — "باید" is the modal "ought/must" and "بگوی" is the subjunctive used here as a hortative/imperative (polite command) meaning "say" or "do say". The subordinate temporal clause precedes the imperative content.
segments.s670-g72: This is a prohibitive/result clause: "نه" (lest / do not) + "زان" (from that) + the doubled/archaic object phrase "مر مراکم" (to me, emphatic/arch. form) + "شود آب روی" ("the water of the face becomes" = tears come to my face). In short: "do not (say) so that, because of it, tears come to me." The archaic duplication (مر مراکم) stresses the effect on the speaker.
segments.s670-g393: نگر is an imperative 'look/see' addressing the listener; تا introduces a subordinate content clause ('see what...'), functioning like 'how/what' here; چه is the interrogative 'what'; گفت is the reported-verb 'said' (the lexical sense list given lacks the verbal sense, so treat it as the past-tense verb); آن خردمند پیر is a noun phrase 'that wise old elder' (آن determiner + خردمند 'wise' + پیر 'old/man'). The whole segment is an instruction to observe the content of that elder's speech.
segments.s670-g394: به ری is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/at Rey' (به + place name). چون here functions temporally/causally 'when/since'; دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his); تنگ شد is a past formation 'became tight/was distressed' (تنگ 'constricted' + شد 'became'); ز اردشیر is a poetic 'because of/from Ardashir' (ز = از 'from/because of' + proper name). Together it gives the time/cause when the elder spoke.
segments.s670-g395: سری پر زکینه is a prepositional nominal phrase 'a head full of hatred' (سری 'head' + پر 'full (of)' + زکینه 'of hatred'). دلی پر زدرد parallels it: دلی is a form related to 'دل' (= heart) used here as the noun, پر 'full', زدرد 'from pain' (ز + درد). This line piles up descriptive noun phrases in a poetic parallelism.

segments.s670-g180: 'بیامد' (بـ + آمد) is a past-form verb 'came/approached.' 'برشاه' is the prepositional sequence 'بر + شاه' meaning 'to/upon the king'; 'ایران' qualifies the king as 'of Iran'; 'دمان' finalizes the phrase as 'the hem/skirt' (literally 'to the hem of the king of Iran'). Word order places the location noun (دمان) at the end for emphasis.
segments.s670-g181: 'درنگی ببد' is an archaic verbal phrase meaning 'he paused/a brief pause occurred' (درنگی = a pause/hesitation; ببد = older past form of بود/شد). 'تا' introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'until.' 'جهاندار شاه' is an appositive/title sequence ('world-ruler, the king') serving as the subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s670-g182: 'نشست' is the past of 'نشستن' ('sat' / in some contexts 'mounted/settled'); here it reads as 'sat (up/on)'. The sequence 'از بر' is an older/poetic prepositional combination with 'بر' indicating position ('on/upon') and 'از' present as part of the idiom; 'تازی اسبی سیاه' is a noun phrase 'Arabian (تازی) horse (اسبی) black (سیاه)' with adjective order typical of Ferdowsi's diction, yielding 'the black Arabian steed.'
segments.s670-g183: 'شهنشاه' is the title 'shahanshah' (king of kings). 'اسب تگاور' is an NP where 'تگاور' is an epithet applied to the horse (literary/archaic epithet meaning roughly 'war‑horse' or 'steed of a rider'); 'براند' (راند) is the past verb 'drove/rode' (he caused the horse to move). Typical Persian places the verb at the end of the clause.
segments.s670-g184: 'بدهلیز' is a fused poetic prepositional phrase equivalent to 'به دهلیز' = 'to/in the vestibule / antechamber.' 'با او' = 'with him' (با + pronoun او). 'زمانی' is an adverbial 'for a while / for a time.' 'بماند' is the (old/poetic) present/past-tense form of ماندن used narratively to mean 'remained / lingered.' The sequence thus means 'he remained in the antechamber with him for a while.'
segments.s670-g33: گرت هست is an archaic conditional construction: گرت (a contraction corresponding to اگر + تو / ‘if you’) + هست (‘is’) gives ‘if there is to you’ = ‘if you have’. با شاه ایران is a prepositional phrase ‘with the Shah of Iran’ placed before the verb. مگوی is the negative imperative/command (‘do not say/tell’); the object/thing not to be told is left implicit and linked to the following clause.
segments.s670-g34: نیاید is a classical third‑person negative present/subjunctive sense ‘does not come / will not come’. تو را is the dative/affected pronoun ‘to you’ (را here marking the person affected). نزد او is a locative phrase ‘in the presence of / before him’. آب روی is a Persian idiom (آبِ روی) ‘the water of the face’ = ‘honor, face/reputation’; here it is the subject/thing that will not come to you in his presence.
segments.s670-g35: بدو گفت خاقان is an inverted word order common in classical Persian: بدو (‘to him/unto him’, dative) + گفت (‘said’) + خاقان (the speaker, ‘the khagan’). که introduces reported speech (‘that’). ما راگله (here read as ما + راگله) means ‘our complaint/grievance’ — راگله is a nominal meaning ‘complaint’. The whole clause supplies the content of what the khagan said.
segments.s670-g36: زبختست is a fused expression zِ + بخت + است meaning ‘it is by fate / it is due to fortune’ (زبخت + است). و is the coordinator ‘and’. کردم is the first‑person past ‘I did / I have made’. به یزدان is an adverbial invocation ‘by / to God’ (here emphasizing the speaker’s action or oath). یله is an adjective/noun ‘loose, slack’ used predicatively — together with کردم it conveys ‘I let it go / I gave slack’ (i.e. I left the matter, resigned).
segments.s670-g37: نه من starts the negation ‘not I’. زان شمارم is a classical idiom ‘I am of that number’ with زان = ‘of that’ and شمارم (present of شمردن/شماریدن) meaning ‘I am counted / I reckon myself’. The negative scope applies to the relative clause introduced by که (‘who/that’). از هرکسی is the source phrase ‘from every person / from anyone’, i.e. ‘on the say‑so of anyone’.
segments.s670-g38: سخنها is the plural ‘words/talks’. همی‑راند is an old habitual/resultative form (‘was driving/was putting forth’ or ‘would spread/propagate’) — here part of the relative clause characterizing those ‘of that number’ (i.e. ‘who spread others’ words’). خواهم is the first‑person modal/future ‘I will / I want’; بسی here is an adverb meaning ‘enough / sufficiently / much’. Together خواهم بسی expresses a concluding personal stance: ‘I will be content / that is enough for me.’
segments.s670-g39: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if’. شهریار تو is ‘your sovereign’ with the possessive pronoun تو placed after the noun in classical style (شهریارِ تو). زین آگهی is a genitive phrase ‘of this news/information’ (زین = ‘of this’ + آگهی ‘news’); the whole clause sets the condition ‘if your sovereign of this news…’ (i.e. ‘if your sovereign does not learn/accept this news’).
segments.s670-g40: نیابد is a third‑person negative/delimitative form of یابیدن here meaning ‘does not find / does not learn / does not accept’. نزیبد is the negative third‑person present of زیبد (زیبیدن) meaning ‘it does not befit / does not please / is not fitting’. برو is the imperative ‘go’. بر is the preposition ‘onto / to’. مهی (adjective ‘moon‑like’ used substantively) here yields the image ‘to the moon’ or ‘to the beautiful/exalted one’ — a poetic instruction to depart or to go to an exalted place. The sequence reads as a conditional verdict followed by a curt imperative.
segments.s670-g297: چوپرموده را دید کرد آفرین functions as a single clause: چوپرموده را is the definite object (را marks the object) — here 'the well‑taught one'. دید کرد is a past action (دید + light verb کرد) meaning 'saw' or 'gave sight to' and in classical usage can take on the light‑verb flavor of 'expressed' when followed by a noun of utterance. آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'praise, bravo' used here as what was uttered; the order is object — verb — what was uttered, typical of older Persian where a noun of utterance follows the verb.
segments.s670-g298: ازو سربپیچید خاقان چین is another clause whose subject is خاقان. ازو 'from him' is the prepositional/agent phrase indicating the person away from whom the action happens; سربپیچید is a past intransitive verb 'turned (one's) head / averted (one's) face'; خاقان is the subject following the verb (inverted order). چین ('wrinkle' / 'frown') is a small nominative/absolute noun after the verb indicating the accompanying facial expression — a compact poetic sequence meaning 'the Khaghan turned his head away from him, a wrinkle appearing (i.e. he frowned)'.
segments.s670-g299: نپذرفت ازو هرچ آورده بود is a negative past clause: نپذرفت is the simple past negative 'did not accept / refused'. ازو 'from him' marks the source of what was offered. هرچ 'whatever/anything' is the indefinite object governed by نپذرفت, and آورده بود is the pluperfect/past‑perfect 'had brought' (آورده + auxiliary بود), indicating the giving action occurred before the refusal.
segments.s670-g300: علفت بود اگر بدره وبرده بود is an elliptical, enumerative continuation giving alternatives of what was offered. علفت بود: predicate 'it was fodder/grass' (علف + copula بود). اگر here functions as 'whether' introducing alternatives; بدره و برده are two nouns joined by و ('a beauty and a slave' in this textual reading) and the final بود repeats the copular sense 'was' to apply to the listed items. This construction is typical of classical Persian lists with a final copula.
segments.s670-g301: همی‌راند بهرام با او به راه is a present‑progressive/stative past narrative clause: همی‑راند (the prefix همی‑ marks continuous or habitual action) + راند (drive) so 'was driving/led'. بهرام is the explicit subject. با او 'with him' indicates accompaniment, and به راه 'on the road' is the direction/place phrase. Word order is Subject after verb (poetic flexibility) with prepositional phrases after it.
segments.s670-g302: نکرد ایچ خاقان بدو بر نگاه is a negated, emphatic clause. نکرد (did not do) with the emphatic adverb ایچ 'at all, nothing' yields 'did not at all'. خاقان is the subject (inverted order). بدو بر نگاه is a compact nominalized phrase meaning 'a look/gaze toward him' (بدو 'to him', بر 'toward/upon', نگاه 'gaze'); together with نکرد it means 'the Khaghan did not at all cast a glance toward him' — note the object 'gaze' is expressed as a noun phrase rather than a finite verb 'looked'.
segments.s670-g303: بدین گونه برتاسه منزل براند: بدین گونه is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in this way / thus' (here written as ب + دین + گونه in the tokenization; together they form بدین 'in this' + گونه 'manner'). برتاسه appears as a place/name (proper or local term) and منزل is the noun 'dwelling / stage (of a journey)' serving as the goal; براند is past 'drove' (راند with prefix ب- in older usage). The clause structure is adverbial phrase → destination → verb 'he drove (them)'.
segments.s670-g304: که یک روز پرموده اورانخواند: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that / for', یک روز 'one day' gives the time. پرموده 'the well‑taught / experienced (man)' is the person referenced, and اورانخواند is a compound past verb meaning 'he called/summoned them' (اوران + خواند, where خواند here functions as 'called' or 'summoned'). The clause reads 'for one day he summoned the well‑taught (man)'.
segments.s670-g417: یکی گور دید اندر آن مرغزار — 'یکی' here is the indefinite 'a certain / one', placed before the noun; 'گور' is the animal (onager) and the phrase 'یکی گور' functions as an indefinite NP. 'دید' is the simple past (3sg) 'saw / beheld'. The locative phrase 'اندر آن مرغزار' ('in that meadow') follows the verb, as is common in epic Persian.
segments.s670-g418: کزان خوبتر کس نبیند نگار — 'کزان' is a relative/comparative particle 'than which / from which' linking to the following clause and qualifying 'نگار'. 'خوبتر' is a comparative adjective 'more beautiful / better' placed before the person-word it compares. 'کس نبیند' is a general negative statement: 'no one sees' (نِـ- prefix marks negation; نبیند here is used as general/present-tense literary 'does not see' / 'has not seen'). 'نگار' is the head noun 'beloved' / 'beautiful one' that the relative phrase describes.
segments.s670-g419: پس اندر همی‌راند بهرام نرم — 'پس' is a temporal 'then'. 'اندر' marks location/scene 'there / in it'. 'همی‌راند' is a literary/progressive-form verb (همی- prefix + راندن) indicating an ongoing action in the narrative: 'was riding / kept riding'. 'بهرام' is the subject (proper name) and 'نرم' is an adverb/adjectival manner 'gently', modifying the verb.
segments.s670-g420: برو بارگی را نکرد ایچ گرم — this clause says that he did not at all disturb the leaves. 'برو' here functions as a contracted locative/prefixal usage ('بر او' = 'upon it / on them') rather than the ordinary imperative 'go' (an archaic usage common in epic texts). 'بارگی را' is the definite object 'the foliage / the leaves' (را marks the definite object). 'نکرد' is past negative 'did not do / did not cause'. 'ایچ' = 'anything / at all' (negated), and 'گرم' is used adverbially as an intensifier (literally 'warm/strong' but here 'in any strong degree'). Together: 'he did not at all (even) strongly affect the foliage' — idiomatically 'he did not disturb the leaves in the least.'
segments.s670-g421: بدان بیشه در جای نخچیرگاه — 'بدان' is a fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) meaning 'to/at that' or 'in that'; in epic Persian such fusions are common. 'بیشه' is 'grove / thicket' (the noun governed by 'بدان'). 'در جای' is a locative phrase 'in the place (of)', and 'نخچیرگاه' names that place as 'the hunting-ground' (a compound noun). The whole segment sets the scene/location.
segments.s670-g422: به پیش اندر آمد یکی تنگ راه — 'به پیش' = 'forward / ahead' (directional/adverbial). 'اندر آمد' is the past 'came into view / entered' (اندر = in; آمد = came). 'یکی' = 'a certain / one' introduces an indefinite noun that follows. 'تنگ راه' is an adjectival phrase with the comparative/qualifying adjective before the noun: 'tight / narrow' (تنگ) modifying 'راه' (path). Word order is typical: directional phrase + verb + indefinite NP as new information.
segments.s670-g423: ز تنگی چو گور ژیان برگذشت — 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. 'تنگی' = 'narrowness / constriction' (the reason or location constraint). 'چو' = 'when / as'. 'گور' = the onager; 'ژیان' (literally 'life') here qualifies the animal (a literary touch meaning 'living' or 'full of life'); 'برگذشت' is past 'passed by / went across'. The clause gives the temporal circumstance: 'because of the narrowness, when the onager passed by...'.
segments.s670-g424: بیابان پدید آمد و راغ ودشت — 'بیابان پدید آمد' = 'the wilderness/desert appeared' ('پدید آمد' = 'came into view / appeared'). The conjunction 'و' links what follows. The final words name the broader landscape revealed; in epic diction 'راغ ودشت' lists related landscape items (here to be read as 'plain/meadow and field' — older stanzaic collocations sometimes use archaic place-nouns). Grammatically this is a simple sequence: NP + verb (appear) + conjunction + further landscape nouns.
